Results are not sent as an assistant message, not injected into the system prompt, and there is no separate submission endpoint -- everything flows through the Messages API.
referenceText: Tool use -- return results in a user message with a tool_result block referencing tool_use_id.

stem: Your client is consuming a streaming (SSE) response from the Messages API with `stream: true`. Which statements about the stream are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Incremental text arrives in `content_block_delta` events
The full assembled message is delivered once in a single `message_complete` event
A stream begins with a `message_start` event and ends with a `message_stop` event
Each token is delivered as its own top-level `token` event outside any content block
You must poll a status endpoint between events to advance the stream
rationale: Anthropic streaming uses SSE beginning with `message_start` and ending with `message_stop`, with text arriving via `content_block_delta` (text_delta) events. There is no single `message_complete` payload -- the client assembles the message from deltas. Tokens arrive inside content-block deltas, not standalone events, and SSE is push-based so no polling is required.
referenceText: Streaming -- SSE sequence: message_start, content_block_start/delta/stop, message_delta, message_stop.

stem: You need to process 40,000 non-urgent document-summarization requests overnight at lower cost. Which Anthropic capability is the best fit?
options: The streaming Messages API with `stream: true`
Prompt caching with a 1-hour cache TTL
The Message Batches API, which processes requests asynchronously at a discount
Extended thinking with a large `budget_tokens`
rationale: The Message Batches API is built for large volumes of asynchronous, non-time-sensitive requests at a 50% discount, ideal here. Streaming only changes delivery of a single synchronous response, prompt caching helps only when large prompt prefixes are reused, and extended thinking increases cost and latency.
referenceText: Message Batches API -- asynchronous bulk processing at a 50% discount.

stem: In the Message Batches API, what is the purpose of the `custom_id` you assign to each request in a batch?
options: To set the order in which requests within the batch are processed
To authenticate the individual request against your API key
To specify which model each request should use
To correlate each result back to its originating request when results return out of order
rationale: Batch results are not guaranteed to return in submission order, so each request carries a `custom_id` used to match a result to its originating request. It does not control prioritization, authentication (handled by the API key), or model selection (set per-request in `params`).
referenceText: Message Batches API -- custom_id matches results back to inputs.

stem: Which mechanism enables prompt caching for a large, reused instruction block in a Messages API request?
options: Setting a top-level `cache: true` request parameter
Adding a `cache_control` field with `{"type": "ephemeral"}` to the content block
Passing a `cache_id` returned from a previous response
Enabling caching once in the Console; no request changes are needed
rationale: Prompt caching is opt-in per content block by attaching `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}`, which marks a cache breakpoint covering everything up to that point. There is no top-level `cache` boolean, no `cache_id` handoff between requests, and it is controlled in the request body rather than a Console toggle.
referenceText: Prompt caching -- mark cacheable prefixes with cache_control type ephemeral.

stem: A developer sets `max_tokens: 4096` and wants to enable extended thinking. Which configuration is valid?
options: Set `thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 4096}` so the budget equals `max_tokens`
Set `extended_thinking: true` as a top-level boolean parameter
Set `thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 2048}` so the budget is below `max_tokens`
Set `thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 8192}` to exceed `max_tokens` for deeper reasoning
rationale: When extended thinking is enabled, `budget_tokens` must be less than `max_tokens`, because the thinking budget is drawn from the overall output allowance; a budget equal to or greater than max_tokens leaves no room for the answer. There is no top-level `extended_thinking` boolean -- thinking is configured via the `thinking` object.
referenceText: Extended thinking -- configure via the thinking object; budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens.

stem: You are building a high-volume, latency-sensitive ticket-classification service where each request is short and simple. Which Claude model tier is generally the most appropriate starting choice?
options: An Opus model, optimized for the most complex reasoning
A Haiku model, optimized for speed and cost efficiency
A Sonnet model, for balanced reasoning on moderately complex tasks
Whichever model offers the largest context window, regardless of tier
rationale: Haiku models are the fastest and most cost-effective tier, suiting high-volume, simple, latency-sensitive tasks like short-text classification. Opus targets the hardest reasoning at higher cost/latency, Sonnet is the balanced mid tier (more than needed here), and context-window size is not the deciding factor for simple classification.
referenceText: Models overview -- Haiku is the fastest/most cost-effective tier; Sonnet balanced; Opus most capable.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol (MCP), primitives differ by who controls their invocation. Which statements correctly describe the control model? (Select 2.)
options: Tools are model-controlled: the LLM decides when to invoke them
Resources are model-controlled: the LLM autonomously writes to them each turn
Prompts are user-controlled: typically surfaced for a user to select and trigger
Tools are user-controlled: only the end user may invoke them
Prompts are server-controlled: the server invokes them without user or model input
rationale: In MCP, tools are model-controlled (the model chooses to call them) and prompts are user-controlled (exposed as commands/templates the user selects). Resources are application-controlled read-only data, not model-written; tools are not user-only; and prompts are not server-invoked.
referenceText: MCP concepts -- tools model-controlled, resources application-controlled, prompts user-controlled.

stem: An MCP server wants to expose read-only reference data (such as file contents or database records) that the host application can load into context, analogous to a GET request with no side effects. Which MCP primitive fits?
options: Tools
Prompts
Sampling
Resources
rationale: Resources represent contextual, typically read-only data identified by URIs that the host supplies to the model -- conceptually a GET with no side effects. Tools execute actions (often with side effects), prompts are reusable templated interactions, and sampling is a server-initiated request for the client to run an LLM completion.
referenceText: MCP resources -- expose read-only contextual data (GET-like, no side effects) to the host.

stem: A team wants their MCP server to offer reusable, parameterized message templates (for example a "summarize-git-diff" workflow) that users can invoke on demand, often as slash commands in the client UI. Which MCP primitive should they implement?
options: Tools
Resources
Prompts
Roots
rationale: MCP prompts are user-controlled, reusable templates (which can accept arguments) that clients commonly surface as slash commands or menu items for the user to invoke. Tools are model-invoked action functions, resources are contextual data, and roots define the filesystem/URI boundaries a server may operate within.
referenceText: MCP prompts -- reusable, user-invoked templated messages, often surfaced as slash commands.

stem: A new engineer joins a repository that uses Claude Code and asks why the assistant already seems to know the project's build commands and directory layout without being told each session. What is the primary purpose of the project's `CLAUDE.md` file?
options: It provides persistent project context — conventions, commands, architecture — that Claude Code loads into memory automatically
It stores the API key and default model so the session can authenticate on startup
It defines a CI pipeline that executes automatically whenever Claude edits a file
It lists paths that Claude Code is denied permission to read
rationale: `CLAUDE.md` is a memory file pulled into context automatically so Claude Code knows project conventions, key commands, and architecture. It is not a secrets/config store (credentials live in environment or settings, never committed memory), it is not a CI definition, and it is not an access-control denylist (that is governed by permissions/settings).
referenceText: Claude Code memory — CLAUDE.md holds project context loaded automatically into the session.

stem: You author a custom slash command as a Markdown file in `.claude/commands/` and want the same template to act on whatever text the user types after the command name. How do you make the command incorporate that text?
options: Reference the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command's Markdown body
Wrap the command body in a `<user_input>` XML tag that the harness fills in
Add a `params:` list to the YAML frontmatter and read `${{ params.text }}`
Do nothing — the user's text is prepended as the first line automatically
rationale: Custom slash commands substitute the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder with the text the user types after the command, letting one template accept dynamic input. There is no `<user_input>` tag convention, no `params`/`${{ }}` templating for basic argument passing, and arguments are not auto-prepended — you must reference the placeholder explicitly.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands — use the $ARGUMENTS placeholder to inject user-provided text.

stem: Your team wants a code formatter to run every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, and it must happen deterministically rather than depending on the model remembering to do it. Which Claude Code mechanism enforces this?
options: A PostToolUse hook defined in settings.json
An instruction in CLAUDE.md telling Claude to format after each edit
A custom slash command a developer invokes after edits
Plan mode, which formats files as part of planning
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness runs deterministically at lifecycle events; a PostToolUse hook fires after tools like Edit/Write, guaranteeing the formatter runs. A CLAUDE.md note is only a suggestion the model may skip, a manual slash command still requires someone to invoke it, and plan mode is read-only and does not modify files.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks — shell commands run at events like PreToolUse/PostToolUse for deterministic automation.

stem: A developer turns on Claude Code's plan mode before a risky refactor. What is the defining behavior of plan mode?
options: Claude researches the codebase and proposes a plan, making no file edits until you approve
Claude performs all planned edits immediately and shows a combined diff afterward
Claude disables tool use entirely and can only chat
Claude creates a new branch and commits its changes automatically
rationale: Plan mode restricts Claude Code to read-only investigation so it can present a plan for approval before touching files. It does not auto-execute edits, it still uses read/search tools (so it is not chat-only), and it does not branch or commit on its own.
referenceText: Claude Code plan mode — read-only research and planning before making edits.

stem: Your integration calls the Messages API and the response returns with `stop_reason: "tool_use"`. What should your application do next?
options: Execute the requested tool(s) and return the results before the model continues
Treat the turn as complete, since no further action is required
Handle it as an error that occurred while Claude ran the tool internally
Increase `max_tokens`, because the model was cut off mid tool call
rationale: `stop_reason: "tool_use"` means the response contains one or more `tool_use` blocks and the model is pausing for your code to run those tools and return `tool_result` blocks. It is not terminal (that is `end_turn`), it is not an error, and truncation would surface as `max_tokens`. The model never executes tools itself — your application does.
referenceText: Tool use — a response with stop_reason tool_use requires the client to run tools and return results.

stem: The Messages API is stateless. To keep a multi-turn conversation coherent, what must your application send on each new request?
options: The full prior conversation history in the `messages` array, plus the new user turn
The `conversation_id` returned by the previous response
Only the new user message, relying on the server to retain earlier turns for your API key
Only the new user message, since the model recalls earlier context automatically
rationale: Because the API keeps no server-side session, you must include the accumulated history (previous user and assistant messages) along with the new turn on every request. There is no `conversation_id` handle, the server does not persist prior turns for your key, and sending only the latest message discards all earlier context.
referenceText: Messages API — stateless; the client sends prior turns to maintain conversation context.

stem: You are streaming a response that includes a tool call and need to reconstruct the tool's input arguments as they arrive. How are those arguments delivered incrementally in the SSE stream?
options: As `input_json_delta` fragments within `content_block_delta` events that you concatenate into the JSON input
As a single fully formed `tool_input` event emitted once the arguments are complete
As `text_delta` events that you parse as plain prose
They are not streamed; tool inputs appear only in a separate non-streamed response
rationale: During streaming, a tool_use block's arguments arrive as `input_json_delta` fragments inside `content_block_delta` events; the client accumulates the partial JSON strings and parses them once the block completes. They are not one atomic `tool_input` event, they are distinct from `text_delta` (which carries prose), and tool inputs are streamable within the same SSE response.
referenceText: Streaming — tool inputs stream as partial JSON via input_json_delta within content_block_delta.

stem: Your service prepends a large, stable instruction block and knowledge base to a changing user question on every call. Which two practices maximize prompt-caching effectiveness in the Messages API? (Select 2.)
options: Place stable, reused content before any variable content so the cached prefix stays byte-identical
Ensure the cached prefix meets the model's minimum cacheable token threshold
Put the frequently changing user question at the very top of the prompt
Attach `cache_control` to every small message regardless of size for broader coverage
Force a new cache breakpoint on every request to guarantee fresh writes
rationale: Caching only helps when a byte-identical prefix is reused, so stable content must precede variable content, and the prefix must exceed the model's minimum cacheable token count (e.g., ~1024 tokens on larger models) to be eligible. Putting the changing question first invalidates the shared prefix, caching tiny sub-minimum blocks yields no hit, and forcing fresh writes every request produces cache writes instead of the cache reads you want.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache stable prefixes above the minimum token threshold; order static content first.

stem: You are building a chat feature on the Messages API. Before the model generates anything, your backend must supply the persona and formatting rules that should govern every turn. Where should these instructions go in the request?
options: In the top-level `system` parameter
As the first element of the `messages` array with role `system`
As a `user` message prepended before the real user turn
As metadata attached to the final `assistant` message
rationale: The Messages API accepts a top-level `system` string parameter for system-level instructions; `system` is not a message role, so an array element with that role would be rejected. Embedding the rules in a user message mixes instructions with user content and works poorly, and there is no assistant-message metadata field for this.
referenceText: Messages API — system prompts are provided via the top-level `system` parameter, not as a message role.

stem: Your application posts a conversation to the Messages API and receives a 400 error. The `messages` array is `[user, user, assistant]`. What is the most likely cause?
options: Roles must alternate between `user` and `assistant`, and the array must begin with a `user` turn
The array must always end with a `user` message
Consecutive `user` messages are allowed only when a `system` prompt is present
Every message must include a `tool_use_id` field
rationale: The Messages API requires roles to alternate and the first message to be `user`; two consecutive `user` turns violate alternation and trigger a 400. The array typically ends with the turn you want answered, but the rule broken here is alternation. Consecutive user turns are not conditionally allowed by a system prompt, and `tool_use_id` only appears inside tool_result blocks.
referenceText: Messages API — conversational turns must alternate user/assistant and start with user.

stem: For one specific request you have defined a `get_weather` tool and need to guarantee the model calls exactly that tool rather than replying with text or choosing among tools. Which `tool_choice` value achieves this?
options: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}`
`{"type": "auto"}`
`{"type": "any"}`
`{"type": "none"}`
rationale: `{"type": "tool", "name": ...}` forces the model to invoke that specific named tool. `auto` lets the model decide whether to use any tool, `any` forces some tool but not a specific one, and `none` prevents tool use entirely.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice type `tool` forces a specific named tool.

stem: While defining a tool for the Messages API, you must decide what belongs in the tool's `input_schema` field. What is its purpose?
options: It is a JSON Schema defining the structure and types of the parameters the model must produce to call the tool
It is the schema for the tool's return value, which the API validates results against
It is the natural-language text the model reads to decide when to use the tool
It maps the tool to the specific model version that supports it
rationale: `input_schema` is a JSON Schema describing the tool's input parameters (names, types, required fields); the model uses it to generate a valid tool_use input. It does not describe return values (conveyed via tool_result), the natural-language usage guidance lives in the `description` field, and it plays no role in model-version mapping.
referenceText: Tool use — input_schema is a JSON Schema describing tool input parameters.

stem: A response returns `stop_reason: "tool_use"` with a tool_use block, and your code runs the corresponding function. How must you return the result so the conversation can continue?
options: Append a new `user` message containing a `tool_result` block whose `tool_use_id` matches the tool_use block's `id`
Append a new `assistant` message containing the `tool_result` block
Pass the result in a top-level `tool_outputs` parameter on the next request
Edit the original `assistant` message to include the function's return value
rationale: Tool results are returned as a `tool_result` content block inside a new `user` message, and the block's `tool_use_id` must match the `id` of the corresponding tool_use block. tool_result blocks do not go in assistant messages, there is no top-level `tool_outputs` parameter, and you never rewrite the prior assistant turn.
referenceText: Tool use — return tool_result in a user message with matching tool_use_id.

stem: A tool your application runs fails because an external API times out. How should you report this to the model so it can adapt?
options: Return a `tool_result` block with `is_error: true` and an error description in its content
Omit the tool_result block so the model retries the tool automatically
Return an HTTP 500 from your call to the Messages API
Set `stop_reason: "error"` in your next request
rationale: A tool_result block supports `is_error: true`, letting you report the failure in content the model can read and react to. Omitting the block breaks the required tool_use/tool_result pairing and causes an API error, your code controls its request rather than the API's HTTP status, and `stop_reason` is a response field set by the model, not a request field.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_result supports is_error to signal tool failures.

stem: You set `stream: true` on a Messages request and need to render output tokens as they generate. Which server-sent event carries the incremental text?
options: `content_block_delta` events with a `text_delta`
`message_start` events
`content_block_start` events
`message_stop` events
rationale: Incremental output arrives in `content_block_delta` events whose delta is a `text_delta` (or `input_json_delta` for tool inputs, `thinking_delta` for thinking). `message_start` carries the initial message shell, `content_block_start` opens a block, and `message_stop` ends the stream — none carry token text.
referenceText: Streaming — text tokens arrive in content_block_delta text_delta events.

stem: While consuming a streaming Messages response, which two events mark the lifecycle boundaries of the overall message, as opposed to individual content blocks? (Select 2.)
options: `message_start`
`message_stop`
`content_block_start`
`content_block_delta`
`ping`
rationale: `message_start` opens the stream with the initial message object and `message_stop` closes it — these bound the whole message. `content_block_start` and `content_block_delta` operate at the individual content-block level, and `ping` events are periodic keep-alives with no lifecycle meaning.
referenceText: Streaming — message_start and message_stop bound the message lifecycle.

stem: A nightly job classifies 40,000 documents whose results are not needed for several hours, and you want the lowest cost per request. Which approach fits best?
options: Submit the requests through the Message Batches API
Fire 40,000 concurrent streaming Messages requests
Concatenate all documents into a single Messages request
Enable extended thinking on each Messages request
rationale: The Message Batches API is built for high-volume, latency-tolerant asynchronous workloads and provides a significant cost discount versus standard requests. Tens of thousands of concurrent requests risk rate limits and give no discount, concatenating everything blows past context limits and mixes documents, and extended thinking raises cost and latency — the opposite of the goal.
referenceText: Message Batches — asynchronous, high-volume processing at a discount.

stem: You build a Message Batches request and assign each entry a `custom_id`. Since batch results can return in any order, what is the role of that field?
options: It is a caller-supplied identifier used to match each result back to its originating request
It selects which model processes that individual request
It sets the priority order in which requests are processed
It is the batch-level identifier you use to poll for completion
rationale: Each request in a batch carries a `custom_id` you supply, and because results may arrive out of order you use it to correlate each result to its input. It does not select a model or set priority, and the identifier used to poll batch status is the batch `id` returned at creation — distinct from per-request custom_id.
referenceText: Message Batches — custom_id correlates results to requests.

stem: Your chatbot resends a large, unchanging system prompt and knowledge base on every turn, and you add prompt caching to cut cost and latency. Which mechanism marks the reusable prefix?
options: A `cache_control` block with `type: "ephemeral"` at the end of the content you want cached
A top-level `cache: true` request parameter
An HTTP `Cache-Control` request header
Setting `tool_choice` to `cache`
rationale: Prompt caching is enabled by adding a `cache_control` breakpoint (currently `type: "ephemeral"`) to the content block marking the end of the cacheable prefix. There is no top-level `cache` parameter, the feature is not driven by the HTTP Cache-Control header, and `tool_choice` is unrelated to caching.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache_control ephemeral breakpoints define cached prefixes.

stem: With prompt caching enabled, you want to confirm your cache is actually hitting. Which response usage field reports how many input tokens were served from an existing cache rather than reprocessed?
options: `cache_read_input_tokens`
`cache_creation_input_tokens`
`input_tokens`
`output_tokens`
rationale: `cache_read_input_tokens` reports tokens served from a cache hit, billed at a reduced rate. `cache_creation_input_tokens` counts tokens written to the cache on a miss, `input_tokens` counts uncached input, and `output_tokens` counts generated tokens.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache_read_input_tokens reports cache hits.

stem: You enable extended thinking on a Messages request. Which constraint must the `thinking.budget_tokens` value satisfy relative to `max_tokens`?
options: It must be greater than `max_tokens` to reserve additional space for the visible answer
It has no fixed relationship to `max_tokens` and may be set to any positive value
It must be less than `max_tokens`, because thinking tokens are drawn from the overall output budget
It must equal `max_tokens` exactly so the two budgets stay in sync
rationale: Thinking tokens count against the output allowance, so `budget_tokens` must be less than `max_tokens` to leave room for the final response. A value greater than or equal to `max_tokens` (options 1 and 4) leaves no room for the visible answer or is invalid, and the two are directly related (option 2).
referenceText: Extended thinking — budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens.

stem: You are running a multi-turn tool-use loop with extended thinking enabled. When you send the follow-up request, what must you do with the `thinking` content blocks returned in the prior assistant turn?
options: Strip them out of the next request to reduce token usage
Pass them back unmodified so the model's prior reasoning context is preserved
Convert them into `user` messages before resending
Summarize them into the system prompt
rationale: When continuing a tool-use interaction with extended thinking, prior thinking blocks (including their signatures) must be returned unaltered so the model retains reasoning continuity. Stripping, converting, or summarizing them (options 1, 3, 4) breaks that continuity and can invalidate the request.
referenceText: Extended thinking — preserve thinking blocks across tool-use turns.

stem: A Messages API response returns with `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`. What does this tell you about the response?
options: The model completed its answer normally within the token budget
The model chose to invoke a tool
The model matched one of the configured stop sequences
The model reached the `max_tokens` cap and the output was truncated before a natural stopping point
rationale: `max_tokens` as a stop_reason means generation was cut off at the output cap, so the response may be incomplete. Normal completion is `end_turn` (option 1), a tool call is `tool_use` (option 2), and a stop-sequence match is `stop_sequence` (option 3).
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason max_tokens indicates truncation at the limit.

stem: You need to send an image together with a question in a single Messages request. How is the image supplied within the user message?
options: As a top-level `image_url` request parameter
As an `image` content block (base64 or URL source) in the same message's `content` array as the `text` block
As a separate message whose role is `image`
By embedding the encoded image bytes in the `system` prompt
rationale: Vision requests place an `image` content block (base64 or URL source) in the same message's `content` array as the accompanying `text` block. There is no top-level image parameter (option 1), no `image` message role (option 3), and images are not embedded in the system prompt (option 4).
referenceText: Vision — images are provided as image content blocks in message content.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, which server primitive is model-controlled, meaning the model itself decides when to invoke it during a task?
options: Resources
Prompts
Tools
Sampling configuration
rationale: In MCP, tools are model-controlled: the model can choose to call them to take actions or fetch data. Resources are application-controlled context (option 1), prompts are user-controlled templates (option 2), and sampling is a separate capability, not the model-invoked primitive (option 4).
referenceText: MCP — tools are model-controlled primitives.

stem: An MCP server needs to expose read-only reference data (files or database records) that the host application can pull into the model's context. Which MCP primitive fits this use case?
options: Tools, invoked through tools/call
Prompts, selected by the user
Roots, defined by the client
Resources, identified by URIs
rationale: MCP resources represent readable data identified by URIs that the host can include as context — the right fit for read-only reference data. Tools are for model-invoked actions (option 1), prompts are user-triggered templates (option 2), and roots define filesystem boundaries rather than exposing content (option 3).
referenceText: MCP — resources expose readable data via URIs.

stem: You want to give Claude Code persistent, project-specific context (build commands, conventions) that loads automatically at the start of every session in a repository. What is the standard mechanism?
options: A `.env` file containing a `CONTEXT` variable
A `CLAUDE.md` file committed at the project root
A message pasted manually at the start of each session
A `README.md` file, which Claude Code loads as memory
rationale: Claude Code automatically loads `CLAUDE.md` memory files into context at session start, making them the standard place for project conventions and commands. A `.env` variable is not read as memory (option 1), manual pasting is not persistent (option 3), and `CLAUDE.md` — not `README.md` — is the dedicated memory file (option 4).
referenceText: Claude Code — CLAUDE.md memory files load project context automatically.

stem: You want a reusable Claude Code workflow that a teammate can invoke by typing `/deploy-check` and that accepts arguments. How do you define it?
options: Register the command under a `commands` key in `settings.json`
Add a shell alias named `deploy-check` to your profile
Create a Markdown file in `.claude/commands/` named for the command that can reference `$ARGUMENTS`
Define it as a hook on the `UserPromptSubmit` event
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files placed in `.claude/commands/`; the filename becomes the command and `$ARGUMENTS` injects passed arguments. They are not registered in settings.json (option 1), a shell alias does not create an in-session slash command (option 2), and hooks fire on events rather than being invoked as named slash commands (option 4).
referenceText: Claude Code — custom slash commands are Markdown files in .claude/commands.

stem: You want Claude Code to automatically run a formatter after it edits any file, without depending on the model to remember. Which feature enforces this deterministically?
options: An instruction added to CLAUDE.md asking the model to format after each edit
A slash command the user runs manually after editing
Plan mode, which formats files as part of planning
A `PostToolUse` hook configured in settings that runs the formatter after matching edit tool calls
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness executes deterministically on lifecycle events; a `PostToolUse` hook matching edit tools runs the formatter every time regardless of model behavior. A CLAUDE.md instruction is only a suggestion (option 1), a manual command isn't automatic (option 2), and plan mode is a read-only research mode that does not format files (option 3).
referenceText: Claude Code — PostToolUse hooks run deterministically after tool calls.

stem: A developer engages Claude Code's plan mode before a large refactor. What is the defining behavior of plan mode?
options: Claude executes edits immediately but logs them to a plan file for later review
Claude researches and proposes a plan, deferring edits and mutating commands until the plan is approved
Claude runs only the test suite and reports the failures
Claude switches to a smaller model to conserve tokens while planning
rationale: Plan mode is read-only: Claude investigates the codebase and presents a proposed plan, deferring edits or mutating actions until you approve. It does not edit first (option 1), is not limited to running tests (option 3), and does not change the underlying model (option 4).
referenceText: Claude Code — plan mode researches and proposes without making changes.

stem: You are building an autonomous coding agent and want to reuse Claude Code's harness — its agent loop, tool execution, permissions, and context management — programmatically rather than reimplementing them. Which offering is designed for this?
options: The Message Batches API
The Files API
The Claude Agent SDK
Prompt caching
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK exposes the same agent harness that powers Claude Code (agent loop, tool use, permission handling, context management) for building custom agents. The Batches API is for bulk asynchronous requests (option 1), the Files API manages uploaded files (option 2), and prompt caching optimizes repeated context (option 4) — none provide an agent harness.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK — build agents on the Claude Code harness.

stem: For a high-volume, latency-sensitive classification task where cost matters more than deep reasoning, which model-selection heuristic is most appropriate?
options: Always select the largest Opus-tier model to maximize accuracy regardless of the task
Select whichever model has the most recent release date
Select the model with the largest context window
Select a smaller, faster Haiku-tier model, which optimizes for speed and cost on well-scoped tasks
rationale: Model selection should match task requirements; for high-volume, latency- and cost-sensitive, well-scoped tasks, a smaller/faster Haiku-tier model is the right trade-off. Maxing out on the largest model (option 1) wastes cost and latency, newest release date (option 2) ignores fit, and context window alone (option 3) is only one relevant dimension.
referenceText: Models overview — match model tier to task speed/cost/intelligence needs.

stem: A developer sends a Messages request whose only turn is a user message. Claude responds with `stop_reason: "tool_use"`. What must the application do next to continue the conversation correctly?
options: Resend the original request unchanged, since tool_use means the request must be retried
Append an assistant message containing the tool_use block, then append a user message with a tool_result block referencing the same tool_use_id
Send a user message containing only a tool_result block, omitting the assistant turn that held the tool_use block
Set tool_choice to {"type": "none"} and resend so Claude answers without the tool
rationale: Turn order must be preserved: echo back the assistant turn (with its tool_use block), then supply a user turn whose tool_result carries the matching tool_use_id and the tool output. Retrying the same request loses the call (option 1), sending a tool_result without the preceding assistant tool_use breaks structure and is rejected (option 3), and forcing tool_choice none abandons the pending call (option 4).
referenceText: Tool use — after stop_reason tool_use, return the tool_use block in the assistant turn and a tool_result with the matching tool_use_id in the next user turn.

stem: In a tool definition passed to the Messages API, what is the purpose of the `input_schema` field?
options: It contains the tool's executable source code, which the API runs on Anthropic's servers
It stores the cached result of the tool's most recent invocation
It is a JSON Schema object describing the tool's expected input parameters so Claude emits a valid tool_use input
It is a natural-language description shown to the end user in the client UI
rationale: `input_schema` is a JSON Schema definition of the tool's parameters that Claude uses to structure the input it emits in a tool_use block. The API never executes tool code — your application does (option 1); there is no result-caching field inside a tool definition (option 2); and the human-readable explanation belongs in the `description` field (option 4).
referenceText: Tool use — each tool has name, description, and input_schema (JSON Schema) defining the input Claude must produce.

stem: An application must guarantee that Claude calls the `get_weather` tool (and no other) on a particular request rather than optionally answering in prose. Which `tool_choice` value achieves this?
options: {"type": "auto"}
{"type": "none"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
rationale: `{"type": "tool", "name": ...}` forces Claude to invoke that specific named tool. `auto` lets Claude decide whether to use any tool (option 1), `none` forbids tool use (option 2), and `any` forces some tool but not a specific one (option 3).
referenceText: tool_choice — auto (model decides), any (must use some tool), tool (must use the named tool), none (no tools).

stem: Your application executes a tool and the tool raises an error. How should you communicate that failure back to Claude in the follow-up request?
options: Omit the tool_result entirely so Claude infers that the tool failed
Return a tool_result block with the matching tool_use_id and `is_error` set to true, placing the error text in `content`
Rewrite the assistant's previous tool_use block to describe the error
Send a new system prompt instructing Claude that the tool failed
rationale: A tool_result block supports an `is_error` boolean; setting it true with the message in `content` tells Claude the call failed so it can adjust. Omitting the tool_result leaves an unanswered tool_use and breaks structure (option 1), you must not rewrite the prior tool_use block (option 3), and a system prompt is the wrong channel and does not link to the tool_use_id (option 4).
referenceText: tool_result blocks include tool_use_id, content, and an optional is_error flag to signal execution failures.

stem: When streaming a Messages response over SSE, which event marks the beginning of each individual content block (such as a text block or a tool_use block) within the message?
options: message_start
message_delta
content_block_start
ping
rationale: `content_block_start` opens each content block and is followed by content_block_delta events and a content_block_stop. `message_start` fires once at the start of the whole message (option 1), `message_delta` carries top-level changes like stop_reason near the end (option 2), and `ping` events are periodic keep-alives (option 4).
referenceText: Streaming — message_start, then per block content_block_start / content_block_delta / content_block_stop, then message_delta and message_stop.

stem: While streaming a response that includes a tool call, which delta type carries the incrementally streamed JSON of the tool's input arguments?
options: text_delta
thinking_delta
signature_delta
input_json_delta
rationale: For tool_use blocks, the arguments stream as partial JSON via `input_json_delta` events that the client accumulates and parses once the block stops. `text_delta` streams ordinary text (option 1), `thinking_delta` streams extended-thinking content (option 2), and `signature_delta` carries a thinking block's signature (option 3).
referenceText: Streaming — tool_use input arrives as input_json_delta partial JSON fragments to be concatenated.

stem: A team wants to process 40,000 independent document-classification prompts overnight and does not need immediate responses. Which statements about using the Message Batches API for this are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Batches guarantee sub-second latency for each request, like synchronous calls
Batch processing is offered at roughly a 50% discount compared with standard per-request pricing
Results must be read from a single JSON field in the create-batch HTTP response body
Batches are processed asynchronously, with most completing well within the 24-hour target window
Each request in a batch must depend on the output of the preceding request
rationale: Correct: the Batches API prices requests at roughly a 50% discount (option 2) and runs jobs asynchronously with a 24-hour completion target (option 4). Batches are explicitly not for low-latency needs (option 1); results are fetched later from the batch's results endpoint, not inline at creation (option 3); and batch requests are independent and processed in parallel, not chained (option 5).
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous processing within 24 hours at ~50% cost savings; results retrieved from the batch's results endpoint after completion.

stem: A developer prepends a large, unchanging 20,000-token knowledge base to every request and wants repeated requests to be cheaper and faster. How does prompt caching mark which prefix to cache?
options: By setting a top-level `cache: true` parameter on the request
By sending the knowledge base once to a separate /cache endpoint before messaging
By placing a `cache_control` breakpoint with {"type": "ephemeral"} on the last content block of the reusable prefix
By adding the keyword CACHE at the start of the system prompt
rationale: You mark a cache breakpoint by adding `cache_control` with type ephemeral to the content block at the end of the prefix you want cached; everything up to that point becomes cache-eligible. There is no top-level cache boolean (option 1), no separate cache-upload endpoint (option 2), and no magic keyword (option 4) — caching is controlled per content block.
referenceText: Prompt caching — add cache_control (type ephemeral) to content blocks to define cache breakpoints for reusable prefixes.

stem: A team enables prompt caching using a standard ephemeral cache_control breakpoint. By default, how long does that cache entry remain valid before expiring if it is not used again?
options: Approximately 5 minutes, with the timer resetting each time the cached prefix is read
Exactly 24 hours from the moment the breakpoint is first written
Until the underlying model version is deprecated
For the lifetime of the API key, until the key is rotated
rationale: Correct: the default ephemeral cache has a ~5-minute time-to-live that refreshes on every cache hit; a longer 1-hour TTL is available as an option. Distractors: 24 hours is not the default TTL; cache lifetime is not tied to model deprecation or to API-key rotation, and ephemeral caches expire automatically rather than persisting for the key's lifetime.
referenceText: Prompt caching default TTL is ~5 minutes, refreshed on cache hits; a 1-hour TTL option also exists.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking on a request. Which parameter caps how many tokens Claude may spend on internal reasoning before it produces the final answer?
options: thinking.budget_tokens within the thinking configuration object
temperature set above 1.0 to widen the reasoning search
top_k raised to a large value to allow deeper reasoning
max_tokens, which also serves as the reasoning-token cap when thinking is enabled
rationale: Correct: extended thinking is turned on with a thinking object of type enabled and a budget_tokens value that limits reasoning tokens. Distractors: temperature and top_k affect sampling, not a reasoning budget; max_tokens caps total output tokens and is a separate limit from the thinking budget (the budget is a subset of, not equal to, max_tokens).
referenceText: Extended thinking: set thinking to {type: enabled, budget_tokens: N} to allocate the reasoning token budget.

stem: A latency-sensitive, high-volume classification feature needs the fastest, most cost-efficient Claude model and can accept somewhat lower reasoning depth. Per the models overview, which family best fits?
options: A Haiku model
An Opus model, trading speed for the deepest reasoning
A Sonnet model, balancing capability against cost
Whichever model advertises the largest context window
rationale: Correct: Haiku models are positioned as the fastest and most cost-effective tier, ideal for high-throughput, latency-sensitive tasks. Distractors: Opus is the most capable but slower and costlier, and Sonnet is the balanced mid-tier; both are over-provisioned for simple classification. Context-window size does not determine speed or cost per token and is irrelevant to short classification inputs.
referenceText: Models overview: Haiku is optimized for speed and cost; Opus for the most complex reasoning; Sonnet balances the two.

stem: A developer wants to build an autonomous coding agent that plans, edits files, runs commands, and iterates, reusing the same agent harness that powers Claude Code. Which Anthropic offering is designed for this?
options: The Claude Agent SDK
The Message Batches API
Prompt caching
The token counting endpoint
rationale: Correct: the Claude Agent SDK exposes the agent loop, tool execution, and context management used by Claude Code so developers can build custom agents. Distractors: the Batches API is for bulk asynchronous inference; prompt caching optimizes repeated prefixes; the token counting endpoint only estimates token usage — none provide an agent harness.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK: build agents on the same infrastructure as Claude Code, including tool use and the agent loop.

stem: A team wants project-specific conventions (build commands, code style, architectural notes) to be available to Claude Code automatically every session without pasting them each time. What is the intended mechanism?
options: Commit a CLAUDE.md memory file at the project root, which Claude Code loads into context automatically
Place the conventions in the repository README, which Claude Code parses on startup
Define them under a 'context' key in .claude/settings.json that is injected into each prompt
Supply them through shell environment variables that Claude Code reads at launch
rationale: Correct: CLAUDE.md is Claude Code's memory file; a project-root CLAUDE.md is pulled into context each session so shared conventions persist. Distractors: the README is not automatically treated as persistent memory; settings.json configures behavior but has no 'context' injection key for prose conventions; environment variables are not read as project memory.
referenceText: Claude Code memory: CLAUDE.md files (project and user scope) are automatically loaded to provide persistent project context.

stem: A developer wants to package a frequently used, parameterized prompt (for example, "/fix-issue <number>") so any teammate can invoke it by name inside Claude Code. What is the correct approach?
options: Create a custom slash command as a Markdown file in the .claude/commands directory
Define it as a custom tool with an input_schema in each Messages API request
Register it as a hook in settings.json bound to a keyword trigger
Document it as an instruction block inside the project CLAUDE.md
rationale: Correct: custom slash commands are defined as Markdown files under .claude/commands (project) or the user commands directory and can accept arguments. Distractors: a Messages API tool input_schema defines API tool parameters, not a Claude Code command; hooks run shell commands at lifecycle events, not user-invoked prompts; CLAUDE.md provides passive context but does not create an invocable named command.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: define reusable commands as Markdown files in .claude/commands, supporting arguments.

stem: A team wants Claude Code to run a formatter automatically after it edits any file, and to block edits to a protected directory. Which feature lets them run their own shell commands at defined points in the agent's lifecycle?
options: Hooks configured in settings.json
The CLAUDE.md memory file
Plan mode
The tool_choice parameter
rationale: Correct: hooks are user-defined shell commands that execute at lifecycle events (for example PreToolUse and PostToolUse), enabling auto-formatting or blocking actions. Distractors: CLAUDE.md provides context but cannot execute commands or enforce policy deterministically; plan mode gates execution but is not a command runner for arbitrary events; tool_choice is a Messages API parameter unrelated to Claude Code lifecycle automation.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks: shell commands triggered at events like PreToolUse/PostToolUse to enforce or automate behavior.

stem: A developer starts a risky refactor in Claude Code and wants it to research the codebase and propose a step-by-step approach for approval without making any file changes yet. Which capability fits?
options: Plan mode, a read-only mode that proposes an approach before any edits are executed
Extended thinking with budget_tokens set to zero
The Message Batches API
Setting tool_choice to "any"
rationale: Correct: plan mode is a read-only mode in which Claude Code investigates and presents a plan for the user to approve before making changes. Distractors: a zero thinking budget merely disables extended reasoning and does not gate file edits; the Batches API is for bulk API inference; tool_choice "any" forces tool use rather than preventing edits.
referenceText: Claude Code plan mode: a read-only planning mode that proposes an approach before making changes.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, a server exposes primitives that a client application can consume. Which of the following are those server-exposed primitives? (Select 3.)
options: Tools (model-invocable functions the server can execute)
Resources (data the server exposes for context, addressed by URI)
Prompts (reusable prompt templates the server offers)
Sampling (server-initiated LLM completions requested through the client)
Transports (the stdio or HTTP channel over which messages travel)
rationale: Correct: MCP defines tools (executable functions), resources (URI-addressed context data), and prompts (reusable templates) as the primitives a server exposes for a client to consume. Distractors: sampling is the reverse direction — the server requesting a completion via the client's model — not a server-exposed primitive the app consumes; transports are the message-delivery channel (stdio/HTTP), not a context primitive.
referenceText: MCP core building blocks include tools, resources, and prompts exposed by servers to clients.

stem: In MCP, which primitive is intended to be application-controlled context data (for example, file contents or database rows) exposed to the client and typically selected by the user or app rather than autonomously invoked by the model?
options: Resources
Tools
Sampling
Transports
rationale: Correct: resources represent data (identified by URIs) that a server exposes for context; they are typically application- or user-selected rather than model-invoked. Distractors: tools are model-invoked functions with side effects; sampling is the server requesting an LLM completion through the client; transports (stdio, HTTP) are how messages move, not a context primitive.
referenceText: MCP resources: expose data/content identified by URIs for use as model context, generally application-controlled.

stem: A developer inspects a Messages API response and finds stop_reason set to "max_tokens". What does this indicate?
options: Generation stopped because it reached the max_tokens limit, so the output may be truncated mid-response
Claude finished its answer naturally and the turn is complete
Claude is pausing to call a tool and awaits a tool_result
The request matched a stop_sequence supplied by the developer
rationale: Correct: stop_reason "max_tokens" means the model hit the configured output-token cap and the content may be cut off; raising max_tokens or continuing generation may be needed. Distractors: a natural completion is "end_turn"; a pending tool call is "tool_use"; hitting a developer stop string is "stop_sequence".
referenceText: Messages API stop_reason values include end_turn, max_tokens, stop_sequence, and tool_use.

stem: A developer building a chat feature wants to give Claude persistent instructions about its role and constraints that are not part of the back-and-forth turns. Where should these go in a Messages API request?
options: In the top-level system parameter
As an extra element in the messages array with role "system"
Inside each tool's input_schema
In the metadata parameter
rationale: Correct: the Messages API takes role/behavior instructions in the dedicated top-level system parameter, separate from the messages array. Distractors: the messages array accepts only user and assistant roles — there is no system-role element; input_schema defines tool parameters; metadata carries opaque identifiers such as user_id, not behavioral instructions.
referenceText: Messages API: the system prompt is provided via the top-level system parameter; messages contain user and assistant turns only.

stem: A developer supplies three tools, and on a single turn Claude returns two separate tool_use blocks in one assistant message. How should the application respond to continue correctly?
options: Execute both tools and return both tool_results, each with its own tool_use_id, in the content of a single following user message
Return only the first tool_result and ignore the second tool_use block
Send two separate requests, one tool_result each, resetting the conversation between them
Reject the response, since Claude may emit only one tool_use block per turn
rationale: Correct: when Claude issues multiple (parallel) tool_use blocks in one turn, the app runs each tool and returns all corresponding tool_result blocks — each matched by tool_use_id — within one user message. Distractors: dropping a tool_result leaves an unanswered tool call; splitting into separate reset conversations loses context and pairing; multiple tool_use blocks per turn are explicitly supported, not an error.
referenceText: Tool use supports parallel tool calls: return all tool_result blocks (matched by tool_use_id) in a single user turn.

stem: A developer is adding a multi-turn chat feature on top of the Messages API. To make Claude aware of everything said so far, what must the developer do on each request?
options: Store conversation state server-side using a conversation_id and send only the newest user message
Rely on the system prompt to carry prior turns, which the API reconstructs automatically
Resend the full conversation history as an alternating sequence of user and assistant turns in the messages array, because the API is stateless
Enable prompt caching so history is retained server-side and never needs resending
rationale: Correct: the Messages API is stateless and retains no memory of prior requests, so the client must resend the entire prior exchange (alternating user/assistant turns) in the messages array each call. Distractors: there is no conversation_id that persists turns server-side; the system prompt does not reconstruct history; prompt caching only lowers the cost of re-processing repeated prefix tokens and does not remove the need to resend the messages.
referenceText: Messages API — conversations are stateless; send the full message history on each request.

stem: When defining a tool that Claude can call, what is the purpose of the tool's input_schema field?
options: It contains the executable code the API runs on the server when the tool is invoked
It is a JSON Schema object describing the expected parameters (names, types, required fields) so Claude generates valid tool_use inputs
It specifies the natural-language format Claude must follow in its final text reply after the tool runs
It is a JSON Schema describing the tool's return value so the API can validate the tool_result before sending it back
rationale: Correct: input_schema is a JSON Schema documenting the shape of the tool's inputs; Claude uses it to produce well-formed tool_use blocks. Distractors: the API never executes tool code (the developer does); input_schema does not govern Claude's prose output format; it describes inputs, not the tool's return value.
referenceText: Tool use overview — each tool definition includes name, description, and an input_schema (JSON Schema).

stem: A developer wants to force Claude to call the tool named get_weather on the next turn rather than reply with text. Which tool_choice value accomplishes this?
options: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
{"type": "auto"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "required", "name": "get_weather"}
rationale: Correct: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"} forces that specific tool. Distractors: "auto" lets Claude decide whether to use any tool or answer in text; "any" forces the use of some tool but not a specific one; "required" is not a valid tool_choice type in the Messages API.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice supports auto, any, and tool (with a name) to force a specific tool.

stem: Claude responds with a tool_use block whose id is "toolu_01A". After the developer runs the tool, how must the result be returned to Claude?
options: As a system message containing the output keyed by the tool's name
As a new assistant message containing a tool_result block that references the tool by name
By appending the output as plain text to Claude's previous assistant message
As a new user message containing a tool_result block whose tool_use_id equals "toolu_01A"
rationale: Correct: tool output is returned in the next user turn as a tool_result content block whose tool_use_id matches the id of the corresponding tool_use block ("toolu_01A"), so Claude can pair result to request. Distractors: it is not sent as a system or assistant message, it is keyed by id rather than name, and it cannot be appended as plain text.
referenceText: Tool use — return results in a user message tool_result block with a matching tool_use_id.

stem: A developer is implementing the agentic tool-use loop with the Messages API. Which statements about that loop are correct? (Select 2.)
options: The API executes the requested tool automatically on its servers and returns the tool's output in the same response
When Claude decides to use a tool, the response's stop_reason is "tool_use"
A tool_use block is returned without an id, so results are matched by tool name
The developer runs the tool and sends the output back in a tool_result block within a new user message
Tool definitions are sent once and then stored server-side for the life of the conversation
rationale: Correct: Claude signals a tool call by ending with stop_reason "tool_use", and the developer must execute the tool and return its output in a tool_result block in a following user message. Distractors: the API does not execute tools itself; every tool_use block carries an id used for matching; and because the API is stateless, tools must be included on each request rather than stored server-side.
referenceText: Tool use overview — stop_reason tool_use signals a tool call the client executes and returns via tool_result.

stem: A developer is consuming a streaming Messages API response over server-sent events (SSE). Which event type carries the incremental chunks of generated text?
options: message_start
content_block_delta
content_block_start
message_delta
rationale: Correct: content_block_delta events carry the incremental token/text updates (for example, text_delta) as the model generates them. Distractors: message_start opens the stream with an initial message shell; content_block_start signals the beginning of a block; message_delta carries top-level changes such as the final stop_reason, not the streamed text.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — content_block_delta events deliver incremental text via text_delta.

stem: Which workload is the best fit for the Message Batches API rather than standard synchronous requests?
options: A live customer-support chatbot that must respond within one second
Streaming a long-form article to a user's browser as each token is produced
Guaranteeing a forced tool call on every interactive request
Classifying 50,000 archived documents overnight, where lower per-request cost matters more than immediate responses
rationale: Correct: the Batches API is built for large, latency-tolerant, high-volume jobs processed asynchronously at reduced cost — a strong match for tens of thousands of overnight classifications. Distractors: interactive chatbots and token streaming require synchronous, low-latency responses; forcing tool calls is a tool_choice concern unrelated to batching.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous, high-throughput processing for non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: A team is deciding whether to move a large, latency-tolerant workload to the Message Batches API. Which two statements accurately describe how the Batches API behaves? (Select 2.)
options: Batches are processed asynchronously and complete within a 24-hour target window, often sooner
Each request within a batch is guaranteed to return within 60 seconds of submission
Batch requests are billed at a discount relative to standard synchronous requests
Batches stream partial token output over SSE as each request finishes
Batch status cannot be polled; results become available only when the entire batch completes silently
rationale: The Batches API processes requests asynchronously within a 24-hour target window (frequently faster) and is priced at a discount versus synchronous requests. There is no 60-second per-request guarantee, batches do not stream tokens over SSE, and developers can poll batch status and retrieve results.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous processing within 24 hours at a discounted price.

stem: A developer repeatedly sends the same large system prompt and reference document across many requests and enables prompt caching. Which statement best describes how prompt caching reduces cost and latency?
options: A prefix marked with cache_control is stored so that cached input tokens are re-read at a reduced price and lower latency on subsequent requests within the cache lifetime
The response payload is compressed so that fewer output tokens are billed per request
The model's final answer is stored and returned verbatim whenever the same prompt is submitted again
Older conversation turns are automatically summarized so the full prompt fits within the context window
rationale: Prompt caching stores a marked prefix of input tokens so repeated requests read those tokens at a reduced rate with lower latency during the cache lifetime. It caches inputs, not outputs — it does not compress responses, does not replay a stored final answer, and does not summarize history.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache_control marks reusable prefixes; cached input tokens cost less on cache hits.

stem: A team has enabled prompt caching but is observing frequent cache misses. Which two practices will most improve their cache hit rate? (Select 2.)
options: Place large static content (system prompt, tool definitions, long documents) at the start of the prompt, keep it byte-identical across requests, and mark the boundary with cache_control
Prepend a unique request timestamp to the top of the system prompt on every call
Position dynamic, user-specific content after the cached prefix so the static prefix stays stable
Randomize the order of tool definitions on each request to distribute cache load
Keep each cached segment below the minimum cacheable token threshold
rationale: Caching keys on an exact, stable prefix, so static content should sit at the front, stay byte-identical, and be marked with cache_control, while variable content is placed after the cached prefix. A per-request timestamp or reordered tool definitions change the prefix and invalidate the cache, and content below the minimum cacheable token count cannot be cached at all.
referenceText: Prompt caching best practices — keep static prefixes stable and place variable content after them.

stem: An engineer enables extended thinking on a Messages API request to improve results on a complex, multi-step task. What does enabling extended thinking do?
options: It automatically doubles the maximum output length of every response
It disables tool use so the model can dedicate its capacity to reasoning
It allocates a token budget for internal reasoning and returns thinking content blocks before the final answer
It caches the model's reasoning so identical prompts skip computation on later requests
rationale: Extended thinking gives the model a configurable token budget to reason internally, surfacing thinking content blocks before the final answer and improving performance on hard problems. It does not simply double output length, does not disable tool use (thinking can be combined with tools), and is unrelated to caching reasoning across requests.
referenceText: Extended thinking — allocate a thinking budget; the model returns thinking blocks before its answer.

stem: A response from Claude is cut off mid-sentence, and the response object reports "stop_reason": "max_tokens". What is the correct interpretation and remedy?
options: The output reached the request's max_tokens limit; raise max_tokens (or request shorter output), and to continue, feed the partial output back and ask the model to resume
The model refused the request for safety reasons; rephrase the prompt to comply with policy
A tool call is pending; return a tool_result block to allow generation to continue
The request was rate-limited; retry the identical request with exponential backoff
rationale: stop_reason "max_tokens" means generation stopped at the output token cap, so the fix is to raise max_tokens or request briefer output, and to continue you resend the truncated content and ask Claude to continue. A safety stop, a pending tool call (stop_reason "tool_use"), and rate limiting (HTTP 429, not a stop_reason) are different conditions.
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason max_tokens indicates the output token limit was reached.

stem: A developer needs the most cost-efficient, lowest-latency Claude model to handle very high-volume, straightforward text classification. Which model family is the best starting point?
options: Opus, because it delivers the highest capability on complex reasoning
Haiku, because it is optimized for speed and cost on straightforward, high-volume tasks
Sonnet, because it is the only model family that supports tool use
Opus, because only the most capable model produces reliable classification labels
rationale: Haiku is the fastest and most cost-efficient family, ideal for high-volume, simple tasks such as classification. Opus is the most capable but is costlier and slower and is unnecessary for simple work, and Sonnet is not the only tool-use-capable family — all current families support tools.
referenceText: Models overview — Haiku targets speed and cost-efficiency; Opus targets maximum capability.

stem: A team wants to build an autonomous agent on top of Claude that manages an agent loop, tool calling, and context. What is the primary purpose of the Claude Agent SDK?
options: It provides a framework for building agents that run an agent loop with tools, context management, and permission controls on top of Claude
It is a UI component library for rendering chat bubbles and message threads in web applications
It replaces the Messages API with a fine-tuning interface for training custom models
It is a hosted vector database service for retrieval-augmented generation
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK is a framework for building agents — it manages the agent loop, tool calling, context, and permissions. It is not a front-end component library, not a fine-tuning replacement for the Messages API, and not a vector database.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK overview — build agents with the agent loop, tools, and context/permission management.

stem: What is the role of a CLAUDE.md file placed at the root of a repository used with Claude Code?
options: It is a lockfile that pins the Claude model version the project must use
It stores API keys and secrets so the session can authenticate to Anthropic
It provides persistent project context (conventions, common commands, architecture notes) that Claude Code automatically loads into the session
It defines CI pipeline steps that run before each Claude Code command
rationale: CLAUDE.md is a memory file Claude Code loads automatically to supply durable project context such as coding conventions, key commands, and architecture. It is not a model-version lockfile, is not intended for secrets, and does not define CI steps.
referenceText: Claude Code memory — CLAUDE.md supplies persistent project context loaded into each session.

stem: A team wants a reusable /deploy command in Claude Code that runs a predefined prompt. How are custom slash commands defined?
options: As Markdown files placed in a commands directory (for example .claude/commands), where the filename becomes the command name
As entries under a "claude-commands" key in package.json
As environment variables prefixed with CLAUDE_CMD_
As compiled plugins that must first be published to a public registry
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files in a commands directory (such as .claude/commands), and the file's name becomes the invoked command. They are not configured through package.json, environment variables, or a required published-plugin pipeline.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands — define custom commands as Markdown files in a commands directory.

stem: A developer wants a linter to run automatically every time Claude Code edits a file, with no manual intervention. Which Claude Code feature should they use?
options: A slash command the user types after each edit
Hooks — shell commands configured to run automatically on events such as after a file-editing tool executes
Plan mode, which runs the linter before any edits are applied
CLAUDE.md, by describing in prose that the linter should run after each edit
rationale: Hooks are user-defined shell commands that Claude Code runs automatically on lifecycle events (for example, PostToolUse after an edit), making them the right mechanism for auto-linting. A slash command requires manual invocation, plan mode gates edits rather than post-processing them, and prose in CLAUDE.md is guidance the model may follow but is not a guaranteed automated trigger.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks — run shell commands automatically on tool/lifecycle events like PostToolUse.

stem: What is the purpose of plan mode in Claude Code?
options: It lets Claude research the task and present a plan for the user's approval without making file edits or running mutating commands until the plan is accepted
It parallelizes edits across multiple files to complete work faster
It permanently disables all tool use for the remainder of the session
It compresses the conversation to save tokens before executing changes
rationale: Plan mode is a read-only research-and-propose phase: Claude investigates and drafts a plan but does not edit files or run mutating actions until the user approves. It does not parallelize edits, does not permanently disable tools, and is unrelated to token-saving compression.
referenceText: Claude Code workflows — plan mode proposes a plan for approval before making changes.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which two statements about the core primitives are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Tools are model-controlled: the LLM invokes them to take actions or fetch computed results
Resources are executed by the model to perform side effects on the host system
Resources are application-controlled data identified by URIs that a server exposes for the client to read as context
Tools are read-only, URI-addressable documents that only the user can open
Prompts are model-controlled functions that execute arbitrary code on the server
rationale: In MCP, tools are model-controlled actions the LLM chooses to call, and resources are application-controlled, URI-identified data exposed to provide context. Resources are read, not executed; tools are executable actions rather than read-only documents; and prompts are user-controlled reusable templates, not code-executing functions.
referenceText: MCP concepts — tools are model-controlled actions; resources are application-controlled context; prompts are user-controlled templates.

stem: A developer sends a Messages API request and the response returns with "stop_reason": "tool_use". What is the correct next step to continue the conversation?
options: Resend the identical request with tool_choice set to "none" so the model finishes its answer
Append the assistant's tool_use content block to messages, then send a new user message containing a tool_result block whose tool_use_id matches the tool_use id
Immediately retry the request, because stop_reason "tool_use" indicates a transient server error
Discard the assistant turn and start a fresh conversation, passing the tool output in the system prompt
rationale: When stop_reason is "tool_use", the model is requesting a tool call. You run the tool locally and return the outcome in a new user message using a tool_result block whose tool_use_id equals the id from the tool_use block; the prior assistant turn must remain in messages. Setting tool_choice to "none" or retrying does not deliver tool output, and putting results in the system prompt breaks the required tool_use_id linkage.
referenceText: After stop_reason tool_use, run the tool and return a tool_result block with a matching tool_use_id in a subsequent user message.

stem: When defining a custom tool for the Messages API, which field describes the parameters the tool accepts and is used by Claude to structure its tool_use input?
options: parameters, expressed as an OpenAPI path object
input_schema, expressed as a JSON Schema object
arguments, expressed as a Python type hint string
schema_definition, expressed as a Protobuf message
rationale: Each tool definition includes name, description, and input_schema, where input_schema is a JSON Schema object describing the expected parameters that Claude uses to produce a valid tool_use input. The other field names and formats are not used by the Messages API tool definition.
referenceText: Tool definitions include an input_schema field: a JSON Schema object defining the tool's parameters.

stem: A developer wants to guarantee that Claude calls a specific tool named get_weather on the next turn rather than answering in free text. Which tool_choice value accomplishes this?
options: {"type": "auto"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
{"type": "none"}
rationale: tool_choice of type "tool" with a name forces the model to invoke that specific named tool. type "auto" lets the model decide whether to use any tool, type "any" forces use of some tool but not a specific one, and type "none" prevents tool use entirely.
referenceText: Use tool_choice {"type": "tool", "name": "..."} to force a specific tool; "any" forces one of any tool, "auto" is default, "none" disables tools.

stem: In a streaming (SSE) Messages API response, which event carries the incremental text or JSON fragments that make up the model's output?
options: message_start
content_block_delta
message_stop
ping
rationale: content_block_delta events carry incremental updates such as text_delta or input_json_delta for a content block. message_start signals the start of the overall message with an empty content array, message_stop signals the end, and ping events are periodic keep-alives with no content.
referenceText: Content blocks stream as content_block_start, one or more content_block_delta events (text_delta, input_json_delta), and content_block_stop.

stem: A team runs a large offline classification job that is not latency-sensitive. Which characteristic of the Message Batches API makes it the right choice compared to standard synchronous requests?
options: It returns every result in under one second by reserving dedicated capacity
It processes requests asynchronously, typically completing within 24 hours, at a reduced cost per token
It guarantees the model will never return a refusal for batched requests
It automatically enables extended thinking on all requests in the batch
rationale: The Message Batches API processes groups of requests asynchronously, with batches completing within 24 hours (often sooner), at a significant cost discount versus standard requests. It is not low-latency, does not change refusal behavior, and does not force extended thinking on.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes asynchronously within 24 hours at a 50% discount versus standard pricing.

stem: Which two statements are true about prompt caching with the Messages API? (Select 2.)
options: You mark the end of cacheable content by adding a cache_control block with type "ephemeral"
Cache read (hit) tokens are billed at a lower rate than uncached base input tokens
Caching requires no minimum prompt length; even a 10-token prompt can be cached
A cached prompt persists indefinitely until you explicitly call a delete-cache endpoint
Prompt caching can be applied only to the system prompt, never to tools or messages
rationale: Cache breakpoints are set by attaching cache_control with type "ephemeral" to a content block, and cache reads are billed at a fraction (about 10%) of base input token cost. There is a minimum cacheable length (for example 1024 tokens for larger models), the cache has a limited TTL (default about 5 minutes, refreshed on use) rather than living forever, and breakpoints can be placed on tools, system, and messages content.
referenceText: Add cache_control {"type": "ephemeral"} to set a breakpoint; cache hits are billed at reduced cost, with a minimum length and a limited lifetime.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking on a request. Which parameter controls how many tokens Claude may spend on internal reasoning before producing its final answer?
options: thinking.budget_tokens
max_tokens applied only to reasoning
reasoning_effort set to "high"
temperature raised above 1.0
rationale: Extended thinking is configured with a thinking object of type "enabled" and a budget_tokens value that caps the reasoning tokens (and must be at least 1024). max_tokens governs total output rather than a separate reasoning budget, reasoning_effort is not the Messages API parameter for this, and temperature does not control thinking length.
referenceText: Enable extended thinking with thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": N}, where budget_tokens must be >= 1024 and less than max_tokens.

stem: Where does Claude Code look for project-level instructions and context that should be automatically loaded at the start of a session in a repository?
options: A .clauderc JSON file in the user's home directory
A CLAUDE.md memory file at the root of the project
The description field of the most recent git commit
An environment variable named CLAUDE_CONTEXT
