rationale: Claude Code reads a CLAUDE.md memory file (project memory at the repo root, plus user memory at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) to load persistent instructions and context at session start. A .clauderc file, commit messages, and a CLAUDE_CONTEXT environment variable are not the memory mechanism.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md files provide project and user memory that Claude Code automatically pulls into context at session start.

stem: A developer builds a /review-pr slash command for Claude Code and wants every teammate to get it automatically when they clone the repository. Where should the command be defined?
options: As a Markdown file in the project's .claude/commands/ directory
As a Markdown file in the developer's personal ~/.claude/commands/ directory
As a JSON entry under a customCommands key in the global ~/.claude.json config
As a documented command block inside the project's CLAUDE.md file
rationale: Project slash commands are Markdown files stored in the repo's .claude/commands/ directory, so committing review-pr.md there shares /review-pr with everyone who checks out the repo. Personal commands in ~/.claude/commands/ are local to one machine; slash commands are not defined via ~/.claude.json entries; and CLAUDE.md provides context, not command definitions.
referenceText: Project slash commands are Markdown files stored in .claude/commands/ and are shared with the team through the repository; personal commands live in ~/.claude/commands/.

stem: A team wants Claude Code to run a secret-scanner that can inspect and block any file write that would persist an API key, before the write actually happens. Which hook event should they configure?
options: PreToolUse, which runs before a tool executes and can block the call
PostToolUse, which runs after the tool completes and can flag the write
Stop, which runs when Claude finishes responding at the end of a turn
Notification, which runs when Claude Code emits a notification to the user
rationale: PreToolUse hooks run before a tool call and can block it (for example via a deny decision or exit code 2), which is required to stop the write before it lands. PostToolUse only runs after the file is already written; Stop fires at the end of a response; Notification fires on notifications; none can prevent the write.
referenceText: PreToolUse hooks run before a tool call and can approve or block it; PostToolUse runs after a tool completes.

stem: A developer wants Claude Code to investigate a large refactor and propose an approach for review before touching any files. What does plan mode provide for this?
options: A read-only mode where Claude researches the codebase and presents a proposed plan without editing files or running mutating commands until approved
A mode that compresses conversation history to reclaim context tokens during long sessions
A mode that permanently disables all tool use for the remainder of the session
A mode that automatically commits and pushes changes after each completed step
rationale: Plan mode is a read-only mode in which Claude analyzes and proposes a plan for approval before taking any mutating action, exactly matching the review-before-changes need. It is not context compaction, does not permanently disable tools, and does not auto-commit.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude analyze and plan without modifying files or executing changes until you approve the proposed plan.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, which primitive is model-controlled, meaning Claude itself can decide to invoke it to take an action during a conversation?
options: Tools
Resources
Prompts
Roots
rationale: MCP tools are model-controlled: the model may choose to call them to perform actions or fetch computed results. Resources are application-controlled context data, prompts are user-controlled templates, and roots define filesystem boundaries the client exposes, none of which the model invokes as actions.
referenceText: Tools are model-controlled functions that the LLM can call; resources are application-controlled and prompts are user-controlled.

stem: An MCP server exposes files and database rows that the host application can read by URI and attach to the model's context. Which MCP primitive represents this application-controlled, read-oriented data?
options: Resources
Tools
Prompts
Sampling
rationale: Resources are application-controlled, URI-identified pieces of contextual data (such as files or database records) that the host reads and provides to the model. Tools perform actions, prompts are user-invoked templates, and sampling is a server-initiated request for the host to run a model completion.
referenceText: Resources expose data and content from servers that can be read by clients and used as context; they are application-controlled.

stem: MCP prompts are described as user-controlled. In a host application like Claude Code, how are MCP prompts most commonly surfaced to the user?
options: As user-invocable commands, such as slash commands, that the user explicitly triggers
As background tasks that run automatically on every model turn
As hidden system prompts the user cannot see or select
As tools the model calls autonomously without user involvement
rationale: MCP prompts are user-controlled templates that hosts typically surface as explicit user actions like slash commands the user selects. They are not automatic background tasks, not hidden system prompts, and not model-invoked tools (which are model-controlled).
referenceText: Prompts are user-controlled, reusable templates that servers expose and users explicitly select, often surfaced as slash commands.

stem: A tool call fails, and the developer must return the outcome to Claude so it can recognize the failure and adjust. How should the tool_result content block signal the error?
options: Include "is_error": true on the tool_result block along with the error content
Set the request's top-level stop_reason to "error" before sending the result
Omit the tool_use_id so the model treats the failed call as skipped
Send the failure in a message with role "system" for that turn
rationale: A tool_result block sets is_error to true and includes the error text so the model sees the failure and can react. stop_reason is set by the API, not the caller; omitting tool_use_id breaks the required linkage to the tool_use call; and tool_result blocks are returned in a user-role message, not a system message.
referenceText: To report a tool failure, return a tool_result content block with is_error: true and the error details, keeping the matching tool_use_id.

stem: A developer is constructing a raw Messages API request by hand. Which statements accurately describe the request structure? (Select 2.)
options: The messages array uses alternating user and assistant roles, and the final message is typically from the user
max_tokens is a required parameter that caps the number of tokens generated in the response
System-level instructions must be the first element of the messages array with role "system"
Each message's content may contain at most one content block
The model parameter is optional and defaults to the newest available model
rationale: The messages array alternates user/assistant roles and generally ends with a user turn, and max_tokens is a required parameter capping generated output. System instructions go in the top-level system parameter (not a message with role "system"), a message's content can hold multiple content blocks, and model is required with no implicit default.
referenceText: Requests require model, max_tokens, and messages; system instructions use the top-level system parameter and messages alternate between user and assistant roles.

stem: A developer must select a Claude model for a high-volume, latency-sensitive workflow where cost per request matters more than maximum reasoning depth. Following Anthropic's model-selection guidance, which choice reflects the right trade-off?
options: Choose a smaller, faster Haiku-tier model, accepting less reasoning depth for lower cost and latency
Choose the largest Opus-tier model, since a larger model minimizes total cost per request
Choose any model and set max_tokens to 0 to eliminate generation cost
Model choice does not matter because Claude models share identical price and latency
rationale: Anthropic advises matching the model to the task: smaller, faster Haiku-tier models suit high-volume, latency- and cost-sensitive work, while larger Opus-tier models suit the most complex reasoning. Opus is not the cheapest, max_tokens of 0 produces no output, and the tiers differ meaningfully in price and latency.
referenceText: Choose the model that fits the task: faster, more cost-effective models for high-volume simple tasks and larger models for complex reasoning.

stem: A developer wants to build a custom autonomous agent using the same infrastructure that powers Claude Code. What does the Claude Agent SDK primarily provide?
options: A framework built on the harness behind Claude Code that handles the agent loop, tool use, and context management
A hosted UI theme editor for customizing the Claude.ai web interface
A drop-in replacement for the Messages API that removes the need for an API key
A billing dashboard for tracking token usage across an organization
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK exposes the building blocks used by Claude Code, including the agent loop, tool integration (including MCP), and context management, for building custom agents. It is not a UI theme editor, an authentication-free API replacement, or a billing dashboard.
referenceText: The Claude Agent SDK provides the infrastructure behind Claude Code for building agents, including the agent loop, tool use, and context management.

stem: A developer streams a Messages response that includes a tool call and needs to reconstruct the tool's arguments. How are the arguments for the tool_use block delivered across the SSE stream?
options: As incremental input_json_delta fragments in content_block_delta events, accumulated and parsed once the block stops
As a single complete JSON object inside the content_block_start event
As plain text_delta events identical to normal message text
As a base64-encoded blob delivered in the final message_stop event
rationale: For a streaming tool_use block, the input is emitted as partial JSON via input_json_delta events inside content_block_delta; the client concatenates the fragments and parses the complete JSON after content_block_stop. The full input is not sent in content_block_start, is not text_delta, and is not a blob in message_stop.
referenceText: Tool use inputs stream as input_json_delta fragments within content_block_delta events, which are accumulated into the final JSON.

stem: A developer submits a job to the Message Batches API and needs to retrieve the outputs. Which statement correctly describes how batch results are handled?
options: Once the batch's processing_status is "ended", results are retrieved from the batch's results URL
Results stream back synchronously over SSE as each individual request completes
Results are emailed to the account owner and cannot be retrieved through the API
Each request in the batch must be polled and downloaded from its own separate endpoint
rationale: Batches are asynchronous: you poll the batch object until processing_status becomes "ended", then fetch results from the batch's results_url as a JSONL stream. Batches are not synchronous SSE, results are retrieved via the API rather than email, and there is one results endpoint for the batch, not one per request.
referenceText: Poll the message batch until processing_status is ended, then download results from the results_url as JSONL.

stem: A Messages API response comes back with stop_reason "max_tokens" instead of "end_turn", and the text is cut off mid-sentence. What is the most appropriate debugging step?
options: Increase the max_tokens value in the request so the model has room to finish, then retry
Lower temperature to 0 so the model produces shorter, deterministic output
Set tool_choice to {"type": "none"} to prevent the truncation
Add a stop_sequences entry for the cut-off word so the API completes the sentence
rationale: stop_reason "max_tokens" means generation stopped at the token cap set by max_tokens, not because the model finished. Raising max_tokens (within the model's output limit) lets it complete. Temperature affects randomness, tool_choice controls tool invocation, and stop_sequences would cause earlier truncation, not completion.
referenceText: stop_reason "max_tokens" indicates the model stopped because it reached the maximum number of tokens allowed by the max_tokens parameter.

stem: While testing tool use, a developer returns a tool result but the API responds with a 400 error stating the tool_result content does not match. Which requirement is most likely being violated?
options: The tool_result block must be in a user-role message and its tool_use_id must match the id of the corresponding tool_use block
The tool_result must be placed in an assistant-role message immediately after the tool_use block
Each tool_result must include the full input_schema of the tool that was called
The tool_result content must be a JSON object and can never be a plain string
rationale: A tool_result block is returned in a user-role message (representing the tool's output) and its tool_use_id must reference the id of the assistant's tool_use block. It is not an assistant turn, does not carry the input_schema, and its content may be a string or a list of content blocks.
referenceText: Return tool results in a user message using a tool_result content block whose tool_use_id matches the id from the assistant's tool_use block.

stem: A developer consumes a streaming (SSE) Messages response and wants to reliably reconstruct the final message and detect problems. Which events should the handler explicitly account for? (Select 3.)
options: content_block_delta events, which carry incremental text or input_json deltas
message_delta events, which carry top-level changes such as stop_reason and usage
error events, such as overloaded_error, which can arrive mid-stream
schema_validation events, which the API emits when input_schema checks fail
cache_write events, which stream each token written to the prompt cache
rationale: A streaming response delivers text/JSON incrementally via content_block_delta, communicates final stop_reason and usage via message_delta, and can surface error events (such as overloaded_error) mid-stream that the client must handle. There are no schema_validation or cache_write SSE event types; validation errors return as ordinary HTTP errors and caching metrics appear in usage.
referenceText: Streaming events include message_start, content_block_start/delta/stop, message_delta, message_stop, ping, and error events such as overloaded_error.

stem: Before running a large job, a developer wants to know how many input tokens a prompt (including tools and system prompt) will consume, without paying for any generation. Which approach is correct?
options: Call the /v1/messages/count_tokens endpoint with the same model, system, messages, and tools
Read usage.input_tokens from a prior response and assume it is unchanged
Set max_tokens to 0 in a normal Messages request and inspect the usage block
Divide the total character count of the prompt by four to estimate tokens
rationale: The token counting endpoint accepts the same fields as Messages (model, system, messages, tools) and returns the input token count without generating a response. Reusing an old usage figure ignores prompt changes, max_tokens of 0 is invalid, and the chars/4 heuristic is only rough and ignores tools and special tokens.
referenceText: Use the count tokens endpoint to determine the number of input tokens a request would use, including messages, system prompt, and tools.

stem: A developer submits a job to the Message Batches API and needs to programmatically know when results are ready to fetch. What is the correct way to determine that the batch is ready?
options: Poll the batch's processing_status until it becomes "ended", then retrieve the results file
Keep the HTTP connection open, since the batch endpoint blocks until all requests finish
Wait exactly 24 hours, since every batch completes on a fixed schedule
Rely on a webhook callback, since it is the only supported completion signal for batches
rationale: Batches process asynchronously; the developer polls the batch object's processing_status until it reaches "ended", then downloads results from the results URL. The request does not block, batches often finish well before the 24-hour target, and polling is a standard retrieval method rather than a mandatory webhook.
referenceText: A batch's processing_status moves to ended when processing completes; retrieve results from the results_url once the batch has ended.

stem: A developer enables prompt caching and wants to confirm from the API response that caching is working across requests. Which usage fields are the correct signals to inspect? (Select 2.)
options: cache_creation_input_tokens, which is nonzero on the request that writes the cache
cache_read_input_tokens, which is nonzero on later requests that hit the cache
cache_hit_ratio, a percentage the API returns per request
cache_ttl_remaining, the number of seconds left before the cache expires
cached_output_tokens, which counts generated tokens reused from the cache
rationale: The usage object reports cache_creation_input_tokens (tokens written to the cache on the first call) and cache_read_input_tokens (tokens served from the cache on later calls); comparing them across requests confirms caching. cache_hit_ratio, cache_ttl_remaining, and cached_output_tokens are not fields the API returns, and caching applies to input, not generated output.
referenceText: Responses include usage.cache_creation_input_tokens and usage.cache_read_input_tokens to indicate cache writes and hits.

stem: A tool implementation throws an exception at runtime. When returning the outcome to the model, what is the recommended way to let Claude recover gracefully within the same conversation?
options: Return a tool_result block with is_error set to true and a message describing the failure
Omit the tool_result entirely so the model retries the tool automatically
Return the exception text in an assistant text message instead of a tool_result
Set stop_reason to "tool_error" in the next request to signal the failure
rationale: A tool_result block supports is_error: true with descriptive content, letting Claude see the failure and adjust (retry, ask for input, or explain). Omitting the tool_result breaks the required tool_use/tool_result pairing, the error belongs in the user-role tool_result rather than an assistant message, and clients do not set stop_reason on requests.
referenceText: If a tool call fails, return a tool_result block with is_error set to true and an explanation so the model can respond appropriately.

stem: During a test, a developer needs to force Claude to always call a specific tool named get_weather, regardless of the model's own judgment. Which configuration achieves this?
options: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
Set tool_choice to {"type": "auto"} and describe the tool as required
Set tool_choice to {"type": "any"} with a system prompt naming get_weather
Remove all other tools from the tools array so only get_weather remains
rationale: tool_choice of type "tool" with a name forces that specific tool to be called. "auto" leaves the decision to the model, "any" forces some tool but not a specific one, and removing other tools narrows the set but still lets the model answer without a tool unless tool_choice forces one.
referenceText: tool_choice {"type": "tool", "name": ...} forces Claude to use the specified tool; "any" forces use of any tool; "auto" lets the model decide.

stem: During load testing, a service intermittently receives HTTP 529 responses from the Messages API. What is the correct interpretation and handling?
options: The request body is malformed; correct the JSON before retrying
The account lacks permission for this endpoint; rotate credentials before retrying
The service is temporarily overloaded; retry the request with exponential backoff
The rate limit was exceeded; wait for the reset window shown in the response headers
rationale: 529 is an overloaded_error meaning the API is temporarily overloaded; back off and retry with exponential backoff. 400 signals a malformed request, 401/403 signal auth/permission issues, and 429 (not 529) signals rate limiting.
referenceText: A 529 overloaded_error means the API is temporarily overloaded; retry with exponential backoff.

stem: A Messages request fails with HTTP 429. Which action best follows Anthropic's guidance for handling this error?
options: Immediately retry in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Respect the retry-after header and retry with exponential backoff plus jitter
Downgrade to a smaller model, since 429 is model-specific
Split the prompt into two requests, since 429 means the payload is too large
rationale: 429 is a rate_limit_error; the response includes a retry-after header and clients should wait, then retry with exponential backoff and jitter. Tight-loop retries worsen it, model choice does not clear a rate limit, and 429 concerns request/token rate, not payload size.
referenceText: On a 429 rate_limit_error, use the retry-after header and exponential backoff before retrying.

stem: A team is building an empirical evaluation to measure whether a prompt change improved a classification task. Which practices align with Anthropic's guidance on developing tests? (Select 2.)
options: Rely on a single anecdotal example that looked good in the playground
Hold the test set fixed and grade the new and old prompts against the same cases
Change the model, prompt, and temperature simultaneously so improvements compound
Prefer many realistic, representative test cases over a few hand-picked easy ones
Use only exact-string matching, since it is valid for every task type
rationale: Sound evals use a fixed, representative test set so variants are compared apples-to-apples, and favor a volume of realistic cases over anecdotes. A single playground example is not evidence, changing multiple variables prevents attributing gains, and exact-match grading fits only some tasks.
referenceText: Build evals with representative test cases, keep them consistent across variants, and match grading to the task.

stem: A developer receives a response with stop_reason "refusal" and no useful content. What does this indicate?
options: The API rejected the request schema; the tools array must be corrected
The model declined to generate the content for safety reasons; the request should be revised rather than blindly retried
The response was truncated by max_tokens and should be re-requested with a higher limit
The prompt cache expired mid-request and the call must be resent unchanged
rationale: stop_reason "refusal" means the model declined to continue generation for safety reasons; review and revise the request rather than retry unchanged. A schema problem returns a 400, truncation is "max_tokens", and cache expiry does not produce a refusal.
referenceText: The refusal stop_reason indicates the model declined to generate a response for safety reasons.

stem: A developer wants reproducible outputs while debugging a prompt so repeated runs are as consistent as possible. Which setting most directly reduces output variability?
options: Set top_p to 1.0 and leave temperature unset
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to converge
Set temperature to 0 to make sampling as deterministic as possible
Enable prompt caching so identical prompts return identical text
rationale: Temperature 0 minimizes sampling randomness, giving the most consistent outputs (though not a strict guarantee). top_p of 1.0 does not reduce randomness, max_tokens only bounds length, and prompt caching reuses input tokens for cost/latency, not to force identical generations.
referenceText: Lower temperature produces more deterministic outputs; temperature 0 is near-deterministic.

stem: When continuing a multi-turn tool-use loop that used extended thinking, a developer needs to pass the model's prior reasoning back. What must be preserved about each thinking block?
options: Only the human-readable text of the thinking block, with the signature stripped
The thinking block converted into a system prompt string
Nothing; thinking blocks must always be discarded before the next turn
The thinking block, including its signature field, must be passed back unmodified
rationale: Prior thinking blocks, including the signature that validates integrity, must be passed back unmodified during tool-use continuation. Stripping the signature or altering the block breaks validation, converting it to a system prompt is incorrect, and the blocks are required, not discarded.
referenceText: Preserve thinking blocks and their signature when passing them back in subsequent tool-use turns.

stem: A Messages request fails validation because the messages array contains two consecutive assistant-role messages. Which rule does this violate?
options: Every message must contain at least one tool_use block
Messages must alternate between user and assistant roles
The first message must always be from the assistant
Assistant messages may not contain more than one content block
rationale: The Messages API requires roles to alternate between user and assistant; two assistant messages in a row is invalid. tool_use blocks are not required in every message, conversations start with a user turn, and assistant messages may contain multiple content blocks.
referenceText: Messages must alternate between user and assistant roles; consecutive same-role messages are not allowed.

stem: A developer wants deterministic, automated checks to run at defined points in a Claude Code workflow (for example, formatting a file after it is edited). Which Claude Code features are appropriate to configure? (Select 2.)
options: Streaming SSE events emitted by the Messages API
Hooks, which run shell commands on events such as after a tool call
The Message Batches API processing_status field
A CLAUDE.md file that gives the agent persistent project context and conventions
tool_choice set to "any" in the global settings
rationale: Claude Code hooks execute shell commands on lifecycle events (e.g., PostToolUse) for deterministic automation like formatting, and CLAUDE.md supplies persistent project context. SSE streaming, the Batches status field, and tool_choice are Messages API concepts, not Claude Code configuration.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks run shell commands at lifecycle events; CLAUDE.md provides persistent project context.

stem: A developer testing an MCP server wants to confirm which callable capabilities Claude can invoke. In MCP, which primitive represents functions the model can execute (as opposed to read-only context or reusable templates)?
options: Resources, which provide read-only contextual data identified by URIs
Prompts, which are reusable, user-triggered message templates
Tools, which are model-invocable functions the server exposes
Sampling, which lets the server request completions from the client
rationale: In MCP, tools are the executable, model-invocable functions. Resources are read-only context addressed by URI, prompts are reusable templates typically surfaced to users, and sampling is a server-to-client request for completions, not a model action.
referenceText: MCP tools are executable functions a model can invoke; resources supply read-only data; prompts are reusable templates.

stem: A developer submits a tool_use turn where the model's generated input is missing a field the tool requires. Which mechanism is designed to define and communicate valid inputs for this class of problem?
options: The stop_sequences parameter, which validates tool arguments
The tool's input_schema (JSON Schema) with required fields defines valid inputs the model is guided to produce
The metadata field on the request, which enforces argument types
The system prompt, which is the only place required fields can be declared
rationale: A tool's input_schema is a JSON Schema (including a required list) that defines valid input shape and guides the model to produce conformant tool_use inputs. stop_sequences and metadata do not validate arguments, and required fields belong in input_schema, not solely the system prompt.
referenceText: Each tool defines an input_schema in JSON Schema specifying properties and required fields.

stem: A developer wants to review an autonomous Claude Code agent's intended approach before it makes any file edits. Which Claude Code capability directly supports this test-and-review pattern?
options: Auto-accept mode, which applies each edit as soon as it is proposed
Setting temperature to 0 so the agent's edits are deterministic
Disabling streaming so no partial output is applied to files
Plan mode, which has the agent propose a plan and hold edits until you approve
rationale: Plan mode lets Claude Code research and present a plan without executing edits until you approve, ideal for reviewing intended changes. Auto-accept does the opposite, and temperature/streaming settings do not govern whether edits are applied.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude Code propose an approach without making changes until you approve.

stem: A request returns HTTP 400 with an invalid_request_error. According to Anthropic's error guidance, what is the correct debugging response?
options: Retry immediately with exponential backoff, since 400 is a transient server error
Rotate the API key, since 400 indicates authentication failure
Fix the request itself (for example a malformed field or invalid parameter); retrying it unchanged will fail again
Wait for the rate-limit window to reset before resending the identical request
rationale: A 400 invalid_request_error is a client error in request format or content and must be corrected before it can succeed. It is not transient (backoff-retry is futile), not authentication (that is 401), and not rate limiting (that is 429).
referenceText: A 400 invalid_request_error indicates a request format/content issue that must be corrected.

stem: A team must classify roughly 5 million short user comments per day into 'allowed' or 'flagged'. The taxonomy is simple, per-request latency must be minimal, and per-token cost dominates the budget. Which Claude model tier is the most appropriate default choice?
options: The Opus tier, because only the most capable model can be trusted with any moderation decision
The Haiku tier, because it offers the lowest cost and fastest responses for high-volume, well-scoped tasks
The Sonnet tier, because it is the only tier that supports structured JSON output for classification
The Sonnet tier, because a mid-range model is always the safest default regardless of workload
rationale: Haiku is the fastest, most cost-efficient tier, ideal for high-volume, latency-sensitive, well-scoped classification. Opus is most capable but costliest and over-provisioned here. Sonnet is not uniquely required for structured output (all tiers support tool use/JSON), and 'always mid-range' ignores the cost/latency goals.
referenceText: Models overview — choose a model by capability, speed, and cost.

stem: A developer wants to reuse a large system prompt across many requests with prompt caching, but their Claude Sonnet system prompt is only about 500 tokens. What happens when they add a cache_control breakpoint to it?
options: The request is rejected with a validation error for using cache_control on system content
The content is cached, but every request still incurs the full cache-write surcharge
The content is not cached because it is below the minimum cacheable prefix length for that model
The cache is created but silently expires after exactly one second
rationale: Prompt caching enforces a minimum cacheable prefix (about 1024 tokens for Sonnet/Opus tiers, 2048 for Haiku). A 500-token prefix is below the threshold, so it is not cached. It does not raise an error (cache_control is valid on system content), and the default TTL is minutes (5), not one second.
referenceText: Prompt caching enforces a minimum cacheable prompt length.

stem: An analytics team has 80,000 non-urgent summarization requests to run overnight and wants to minimize cost. Real-time results are not needed. Which approach best matches this workload?
options: Send all 80,000 requests concurrently to the synchronous Messages API to trigger parallel-volume discounts
Submit the requests through the Message Batches API for a 50% token discount with asynchronous processing
Enable extended thinking on every request, since additional thinking tokens lower total cost
Disable streaming and cap max_tokens at 1 to make each summary cheaper
rationale: The Message Batches API is built for large, non-time-sensitive workloads and gives a 50% discount on input and output tokens with asynchronous processing (typically within 24 hours). Concurrency on the synchronous API offers no volume discount, extended thinking adds cost, and max_tokens of 1 truncates the summaries.
referenceText: Message Batches API — 50% cost savings for asynchronous batches.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking and sets max_tokens to 4096. Which constraint must the thinking budget_tokens value satisfy?
options: budget_tokens must equal max_tokens so the entire response is reasoning
budget_tokens must be at least 1024 and strictly less than max_tokens
budget_tokens must be exactly 512 regardless of the max_tokens value
budget_tokens is ignored because thinking length is fixed by the model
rationale: Extended thinking requires budget_tokens of at least 1024, and because thinking tokens count toward the response it must be less than max_tokens to leave room for the visible answer. It cannot equal max_tokens, is not fixed at 512, and is not ignored.
referenceText: Extended thinking — setting the thinking budget (min 1024, below max_tokens).

stem: An application must call a specific validation tool named 'validate_address' on every request and never respond with free text on that turn. Which tool_choice setting enforces this?
options: {"type": "auto"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "tool", "name": "validate_address"}
{"type": "none"}
rationale: tool_choice {"type": "tool", "name": ...} forces the model to call that specific named tool. {"type": "auto"} lets the model decide, {"type": "any"} forces some tool but not a specific one, and {"type": "none"} prevents all tool use.
referenceText: Tool use — forcing a specific tool with tool_choice type 'tool'.

stem: While streaming a response that includes a tool call, a client must reconstruct the tool's JSON input incrementally. Which streaming delta type carries the partial tool input?
options: message_delta events of type tool_input
content_block_delta events of type input_json_delta
content_block_start events of type text_delta
message_start events that carry the complete tool arguments
rationale: Tool input arrives as content_block_delta events with type input_json_delta, whose partial_json fragments are concatenated into the tool's JSON input. message_delta carries top-level changes like stop_reason, content_block_start only signals a block's beginning, and message_start does not carry tool arguments.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — input_json_delta carries partial tool input.

stem: A developer is estimating prompt-caching savings for a workload with a large, stable context reused across many requests. Relative to the base input token price, what does a cache read (cache hit) approximately cost?
options: About 125% of the base input token price
About the same as the base input token price
About 10% of the base input token price
About 50% of the base output token price
rationale: Cache reads cost about 10% (0.1x) of the base input token price, which makes caching valuable for repeated large contexts. The ~125% figure is the cache-write surcharge for the default TTL, reads are not full price, and cache pricing is based on input, not output, token rates.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache reads cost roughly 10% of base input token price.

stem: A response returns with stop_reason set to 'max_tokens'. What does this indicate, and what is the appropriate handling?
options: The model completed a full answer normally; no action is needed
The output was truncated at the max_tokens limit; raise max_tokens or continue the generation
The model refused the request for safety reasons; rephrase the prompt
A tool call is pending; return a tool_result and continue
rationale: stop_reason 'max_tokens' means generation was cut off at the token cap and output may be incomplete; raise max_tokens or continue. 'end_turn' is a natural complete stop, a safety refusal surfaces as 'refusal', and a pending tool call is signaled by 'tool_use'.
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason 'max_tokens' indicates truncation at the token limit.

stem: You are structuring a Messages API request to maximize prompt caching. Which parts of the request prefix can carry a cache_control breakpoint? (Select 3.)
options: The tools array
The system prompt
The messages array
The model identifier field
The metadata field
rationale: Cache breakpoints can be attached to the tools definitions, the system prompt, and messages content; together these form the cacheable prefix in the order tools, then system, then messages. The model identifier is a scalar routing field, not cacheable content, and metadata carries request-tracking fields that are not part of the cacheable prefix.
referenceText: Prompt caching — the cacheable prefix is composed of tools, system, and messages, in that order.

stem: Your team is evaluating the Message Batches API for a large offline job. Which two statements about it are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Batches are processed asynchronously and typically complete within 24 hours
It provides roughly a 50% discount on input and output tokens versus standard requests
Each request in a batch returns a live server-sent-events stream as it runs
It guarantees sub-second per-request latency for interactive use
rationale: The Batches API processes requests asynchronously, typically within the 24-hour window, and applies a ~50% token discount. It is not a streaming interface—results are retrieved after processing completes—and it is explicitly not intended for real-time, low-latency workloads, so there is no sub-second guarantee.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous processing within 24 hours at a 50% cost reduction.

stem: A chat feature produces long responses. Users report the UI feels frozen while waiting, and some requests hit an HTTP client timeout before completing. Which single change most directly addresses both symptoms?
options: Switch to the Batches API so the responses are returned asynchronously overnight
Lower the temperature to 0 so the model generates the full response in fewer steps
Enable streaming so tokens render incrementally and the HTTP connection stays active
Reduce max_tokens sharply so every response returns before the client timeout fires
rationale: Streaming delivers tokens incrementally over SSE, improving perceived responsiveness (time-to-first-token) and keeping the connection active, which mitigates client timeouts on long generations. The Batches API is asynchronous and unsuited to interactive chat. Temperature affects sampling, not generation speed. Cutting max_tokens truncates the answer rather than solving the latency of a legitimately long response.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — incremental delivery improves perceived latency and keeps long requests from timing out.

stem: A developer relies on prompt caching but observes cache misses whenever requests are spaced several minutes apart during quiet periods. Given default caching behavior, what is the most likely cause?
options: Cache entries are scoped per organization, and another request in the account evicted this entry
The default cache lifetime is about 5 minutes and refreshes on each hit, so a gap longer than the TTL lets the entry expire
Cache entries never expire on their own, so the misses must indicate an SDK serialization bug
The cached prefix must be re-declared with a fresh cache_control block every hour or it is dropped
rationale: The default cache TTL is about 5 minutes and refreshes each time the cached prefix is read; when requests are spaced beyond the TTL, the entry expires and the next call misses (an optional 1-hour TTL exists). Caches are not permanent, are not evicted by unrelated same-account traffic in normal use, and do not require hourly re-declaration.
referenceText: Prompt caching — default cache lifetime is ~5 minutes, refreshed on each use.

stem: An agent uses extended thinking together with tool use across multiple turns. When it sends a tool_result back to continue the loop, what must it do with the prior assistant turn's thinking blocks to preserve reasoning continuity?
options: Strip the thinking blocks before replying, since internal reasoning should not be sent back to the API
Summarize the thinking blocks into a shorter form to conserve output tokens before resending
Move the thinking blocks into the system prompt so the reasoning persists across turns
Return the prior assistant turn's thinking blocks, including their signatures, unmodified in the conversation history
rationale: When continuing a tool-use loop with extended thinking, the previous assistant turn's thinking blocks—including their cryptographic signatures—must be passed back unmodified so the model preserves its reasoning and signature verification succeeds. Stripping, summarizing, or relocating them breaks that verification and continuity.
referenceText: Extended thinking — preserve thinking blocks (with signatures) when continuing a tool-use conversation.

stem: A product handles a small number of hard legal-analysis requests where answer quality matters far more than cost or latency. Which model-selection rationale is most appropriate?
options: Choose Haiku, since the lowest-cost tier is the safest default and quality gaps can be closed with prompt engineering
Choose Sonnet and raise max_tokens, since a larger output budget compensates for reasoning depth
Choose Opus, accepting higher cost and latency in exchange for the strongest reasoning capability
Choose Haiku but set temperature to 0, since deterministic output improves complex legal reasoning
rationale: For low-volume, high-difficulty reasoning where quality dominates, the Opus tier is the right trade-off despite higher cost and latency. Haiku optimizes for speed and cost, not peak reasoning. Raising max_tokens only allows a longer answer, not deeper capability, and lowering temperature affects randomness, not reasoning strength.
referenceText: Models overview — match the most capable tier (Opus) to the hardest reasoning tasks.

stem: For one specific request, a developer wants the model to answer directly in text and be prevented from calling any of the provided tools. Which tool_choice value achieves this?
options: {"type": "auto"}
{"type": "none"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "tool", "name": "answer"}
rationale: {"type": "none"} disables tool use so the model must respond in text. {"type": "auto"} (the default when tools are present) lets the model decide, {"type": "any"} forces some tool to be used, and {"type": "tool", "name": ...} forces a specific tool—the opposite of preventing tool use.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice: none prevents the model from calling any tool.

stem: A nightly batch job sends the same 30,000-token reference document as context for tens of thousands of requests. Which approach best reduces cost?
options: Send the requests synchronously and rely on the model to internally deduplicate the repeated document
Resend the document each request on the synchronous Messages API, since caching cannot be combined with batching
Submit through the Batches API for the 50% token discount and add a cache_control breakpoint so the shared document is cached across requests
Split the document into many sub-1024-token fragments so each fragment caches independently
rationale: Prompt caching can be combined with the Batches API: the batch discount lowers per-token cost while a cache_control breakpoint on the stable 30k-token prefix avoids repeatedly paying full input price for it. The model does not deduplicate context across separate requests, caching and batching do combine, and fragmenting below the ~1024-token minimum prevents caching entirely.
referenceText: Prompt caching can be used together with the Message Batches API to compound savings.

stem: A developer wants to cache several distinct segments of a prompt—the tools, a long system prompt, and a large document in the first user message—as independent prefixes. What is the maximum number of cache breakpoints allowed in a single request?
options: Exactly 1 cache_control breakpoint per request
Exactly 10 cache_control breakpoints per request
Unlimited breakpoints, one per content block
Up to 4 cache_control breakpoints per request
rationale: A request may define up to 4 cache breakpoints, enabling separate prefixes such as tools, system, and message content to be cached independently. It is not limited to one, is not unlimited, and the documented maximum is 4, not 10.
referenceText: Prompt caching — a request supports up to 4 cache breakpoints.

stem: When consuming a streamed Messages response over SSE, which event signals that the entire message is complete and the stream will end?
options: content_block_stop
message_delta
message_stop
message_start
rationale: message_stop is the terminal event indicating the whole message is finished. content_block_stop ends a single content block, message_delta carries top-level updates such as stop_reason and usage but is not terminal, and message_start opens the stream.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — message_stop is the final event in the stream.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking and also tries to force a specific tool with tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": ...}. What is the actual behavior and supported configuration?
options: Both work together, and forcing a specific tool constrains and improves the model's thinking
Forced tool choice is not supported with extended thinking; only tool_choice auto or none may be used
Extended thinking is silently disabled for that request so the forced tool can be invoked
Forced tool choice is allowed only when temperature is also lowered to 0
rationale: Extended thinking with tool use supports only tool_choice {"type": "auto"} (default) or {"type": "none"}; forcing a specific tool—or {"type": "any"}—is not compatible. The features do not silently disable one another, and extended thinking additionally requires temperature to remain at its default of 1, not 0.
referenceText: Extended thinking — only tool_choice auto or none is supported when combined with tool use.

stem: Profiling shows a request's cacheable prefix is being written on nearly every call rather than read. Relative to base input pricing, what is the approximate cost of a cache write at the default 5-minute TTL, and what does that imply?
options: A write is free, so caching lowers cost even at a 100% miss rate
A write costs the same ~10% of base input as a read, so the write/read ratio does not matter
A write costs about 125% of base input at the 5-minute TTL, so caching pays off only when the prefix is read enough times to offset the write premium
A write costs about 200% of base input at the 5-minute TTL, so short TTLs should be avoided entirely
rationale: A cache write at the default 5-minute TTL costs about 25% more than base input (~1.25x), while reads cost roughly 10% (~0.1x). Caching only saves money when a written prefix is read enough times to recover the write premium, so a write-dominated workload can cost more than not caching. The ~2x figure applies to the optional 1-hour TTL write, not the 5-minute default.
referenceText: Prompt caching — 5-minute cache writes cost ~1.25x base input; reads cost ~0.1x.

stem: A content-moderation pipeline labels millions of short user comments per day as "safe" or "flagged." The task is simple pattern matching that smaller models handle accurately, and per-request latency plus per-token cost dominate the bill. Which model tier best fits this workload?
options: Claude Opus, since the most capable tier yields the highest label accuracy and should anchor any production pipeline
Claude Haiku, the fastest and most cost-effective tier, well suited to high-volume, well-scoped tasks
Claude Sonnet, since balanced mid-tier models are the standard choice for classification workloads
The tier with the largest context window, to fit as many comments per request as possible
rationale: Haiku is the fastest, lowest-cost tier and is the recommended choice for simple, high-volume tasks with strong latency and cost sensitivity. Opus is the most capable but most expensive tier and is overkill here. Sonnet is not required for classification, and context-window size is irrelevant for short comments and does not drive tier selection.
referenceText: Choosing a model — Haiku is the fastest and most cost-effective tier for high-volume, simpler tasks.

stem: A developer enables prompt caching by attaching a cache_control block to a large system prompt. What value must the block's type field be set to?
options: "persistent"
"static"
"ephemeral"
"long_term"
rationale: Prompt caching is enabled by adding a cache_control block with "type": "ephemeral" at the desired breakpoint. The other values are not valid cache_control types; caching is inherently short-lived (a ~5-minute default TTL refreshed on use), which is why the type is named ephemeral.
referenceText: Prompt caching — add "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"} to mark a cacheable prefix.

stem: A data team must summarize 500,000 archived support tickets overnight. Results are not needed in real time and the team wants to minimize cost. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Send 500,000 synchronous Messages API requests concurrently to finish as fast as possible
Enable streaming so partial summaries for each ticket arrive sooner
Use extended thinking on every request to reduce the total number of requests needed
Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes requests asynchronously at roughly a 50% token discount
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, latency-tolerant, asynchronous workloads and offers roughly a 50% cost reduction, making it ideal for overnight bulk summarization. Firing hundreds of thousands of synchronous requests is costlier and rate-limited, extended thinking adds tokens without reducing request count, and streaming only improves perceived latency for a single request with no cost savings.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous bulk processing at a ~50% discount within 24 hours.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking and sets budget_tokens in the thinking configuration. What is the required relationship between budget_tokens and max_tokens?
options: budget_tokens must exceed max_tokens to reserve dedicated space for reasoning
budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens, since thinking tokens count toward the output allowance
budget_tokens must equal max_tokens so the full output budget is reserved for thinking
budget_tokens is independent of max_tokens and carries no ordering constraint
rationale: The thinking budget is drawn from the model's output token allotment, so budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens, leaving room for the visible response. It cannot exceed or equal max_tokens (which would leave no room for the answer) and is not independent of it. The minimum budget is 1024 tokens.
referenceText: Extended thinking — budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens; minimum 1024.

stem: While consuming a streaming (SSE) Messages response, which event type carries the incremental chunks of generated text as they are produced?
options: message_start
content_block_stop
message_stop
content_block_delta
rationale: Incremental text (and input_json for tool calls) arrives via content_block_delta events, each carrying a delta such as a text_delta. message_start opens the stream with an initial message shell, content_block_stop signals a block is complete, and message_stop ends the stream; none of these carry the streamed text fragments.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — content_block_delta events append incremental text via text_delta.

stem: An application reuses a large, unchanging tool schema and system prompt on every request and wants to maximize prompt-cache hit rates. Which two statements about how prompt caching works are correct? (Select 2.)
options: The cache matches on an exact prefix, so cached content should be placed first and kept byte-for-byte stable across requests
Placing dynamic content before the static content improves hit rates by isolating the changing tokens
Cache reads are billed at a fraction of the base input token price, making prefix reuse cheaper than resending it uncached
Cached prefixes persist indefinitely until explicitly deleted through an API call
Cached tokens are compressed and no longer count toward the model's context window
rationale: Prompt caching is prefix-based: a hit requires the beginning of the prompt to match exactly, so stable content belongs first and must not change. Cache reads cost about 0.1x the base input rate, so reusing a stable prefix is much cheaper than resending it. Placing dynamic content first breaks the shared prefix and prevents hits, caches are ephemeral rather than indefinite, and cached tokens still count toward the context window—caching does not compress context.
referenceText: Prompt caching references the longest matching prefix; put static content first, and cache reads cost a fraction of base input.

stem: A workload sends a short static system prompt of only a few hundred tokens on every request and adds a cache breakpoint to it, but never sees cache hits. Why?
options: System prompts cannot be cached; only tool definitions qualify for a cache breakpoint
Prompt caching is available only through the Batches API, which this request does not use
The prompt is below the minimum cacheable prefix length, so it is too short to cache
Caching applies only to output tokens, so a system-prompt input cannot be cached
rationale: Prompt caching enforces a minimum cacheable prefix length (about 1024 tokens for many models, higher for smaller ones); a few-hundred-token prompt is too short to cache and silently yields no hit, with cache_creation_input_tokens staying 0. System prompts, tools, and messages are all cacheable, caching works on standard Messages requests, and it applies to input prefix tokens, not output.
referenceText: Prompt caching — prefixes shorter than the model's minimum length are not cached.

stem: With three tools defined, for one specific step a developer must guarantee that the model calls the get_weather tool rather than replying with text or choosing a different tool. How should tool_choice be configured?
options: Set tool_choice to {"type": "auto"} and let the model decide which tool to use
Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"} to force that specific tool
Set tool_choice to {"type": "none"} so no other tool can be selected
Omit tool_choice, since declaring tools already forces the first one listed
rationale: tool_choice set to {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"} forces the model to invoke that exact tool. auto lets the model decide (no guarantee), none prevents any tool call entirely, and omitting tool_choice defaults to auto—declaring tools does not force the first one.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice {"type": "tool", "name": ...} forces use of a specific tool.

stem: A nightly enrichment pipeline sends the same 8,000-token instruction-and-schema prefix ahead of each of 200,000 documents. The job runs overnight and is not latency-sensitive. Which two changes most directly reduce its token cost? (Select 2.)
options: Enable extended thinking on every request so the model resolves each document in fewer output tokens
Apply prompt caching to the large stable prefix so the repeated prefix tokens bill at the reduced cache-read rate
Switch to streaming responses so total token consumption drops
Submit the requests through the Message Batches API to obtain the asynchronous batch discount
Raise max_tokens to a high ceiling so the job needs fewer requests overall
rationale: Correct: prompt caching lets the repeated 8,000-token prefix bill at the ~0.1x cache-read rate, and the Batches API gives a 50% discount well-suited to this latency-tolerant bulk job; the two compound. Wrong: extended thinking adds reasoning tokens and raises cost; streaming changes delivery timing, not total tokens or price; a higher max_tokens only caps output length and neither reduces request count nor cost.
referenceText: Prompt caching and the Message Batches API can be combined to reduce cost for repetitive, latency-tolerant workloads.

stem: You run extended thinking together with tool use in a multi-turn agent loop. Claude returns a thinking block and a tool_use block. When you send the tool_result back on the next request, how must the prior thinking block be handled?
options: Strip out the thinking blocks before the next request to save tokens
Include the original thinking block(s) unmodified in the assistant turn alongside the tool_use so the reasoning context is preserved
Convert the thinking block into a user message so the model can re-read its own reasoning
Replace the thinking block with a short summary you write yourself
rationale: Correct: with extended thinking and tool use the thinking blocks (including their signatures) must be passed back unmodified in the assistant turn so the model can continue its reasoning chain. Wrong: stripping them, converting them to a user message, or rewriting them all break the signed reasoning chain and cause errors or degraded results.
referenceText: When using extended thinking with tool use, pass the thinking blocks back to the API unmodified, preserving them in the assistant turn.

stem: A Messages API response comes back with "stop_reason": "max_tokens". What does this tell you, and what is the appropriate follow-up?
options: The model completed its answer naturally and no action is required
The model paused to call a tool, so you should execute the tool and continue
The output was truncated at the max_tokens limit, so raise the limit or continue the generation if a complete answer is required
A stop sequence was triggered, so the request must be resubmitted with different content
rationale: Correct: stop_reason max_tokens means generation was cut off at the configured output limit and may be incomplete, so you increase max_tokens or continue. Wrong: natural completion is end_turn, a tool call is tool_use, and a stop sequence is stop_sequence, each a distinct stop_reason value.
referenceText: stop_reason of max_tokens indicates the response was truncated at the max_tokens limit; end_turn, tool_use, and stop_sequence represent other completion states.

stem: A cached prompt prefix is reused by requests that arrive roughly every two minutes all day. Regarding the default cache lifetime, which statement is accurate?
options: The default cache lives about 24 hours regardless of traffic
The default TTL is about 5 minutes and is refreshed on each use, so steady 2-minute traffic keeps the entry warm
The entry expires after a single read and cannot be reused
The cached prefix persists indefinitely unless the model version changes
rationale: Correct: the default TTL is ~5 minutes and every cache hit refreshes the timer, so 2-minute traffic keeps it alive. Wrong: it is not a fixed 24-hour lifetime, it survives beyond one read, and it does eventually expire (a 1-hour TTL option exists but is not the default).
referenceText: The default cache has a 5-minute time to live, refreshed each time the cached content is used; a 1-hour TTL is also available.

stem: A chat product wants users to see the first words of Claude's reply sooner, even though total generation time will not change. Which optimization most directly addresses this?
options: Increase max_tokens so the model has more room to respond
Move the endpoint to the Message Batches API for asynchronous processing
Enable streaming so tokens are delivered incrementally, lowering time-to-first-token
Add more few-shot examples to the prompt so the model answers faster
rationale: Correct: streaming emits output token-by-token over SSE, so users see text almost immediately, cutting time-to-first-token and improving perceived latency even when total time is unchanged. Wrong: raising max_tokens does not speed delivery; the Batches API is asynchronous and slower; adding examples lengthens the prompt and tends to increase latency.
referenceText: Streaming responses improves perceived latency by delivering tokens as they are generated rather than waiting for the full completion.

stem: A team is building a long-horizon coding agent that must plan across many files, orchestrate multiple tools, and reason through complex trade-offs, where accuracy matters far more than per-token cost. Which model tier is the best default starting point?
options: Start with Haiku, the cheapest tier, and move up only if evaluations reveal quality gaps
Start with Opus, the most capable tier for complex, long-horizon agentic reasoning
Start with Sonnet, since the mid tier is the safe default for any workload
Start with whichever tier has the largest context window, since multi-file planning needs the most context
rationale: Correct: Opus is the most capable tier and is recommended for complex, multi-step, agentic work where quality dominates cost; teams can later downshift specific sub-tasks after evaluation. Wrong: defaulting to Haiku sacrifices the reasoning quality this workload needs, a mid-tier default ignores task demands, and context-window size alone does not determine reasoning capability.
referenceText: Opus is the most intelligent model, best for complex, agentic, and reasoning-heavy tasks; smaller tiers trade capability for speed and cost.

stem: An application must feed very large documents to Claude while controlling cost and staying within the context window. Which two strategies are sound? (Select 2.)
options: Send the full corpus on every request to guarantee completeness, regardless of token cost
Retrieve and include only the passages relevant to the user's query rather than the entire corpus
Set max_tokens higher than the model's context window to expand how much input it can read
Cache the large, unchanging document prefix so repeated requests reuse it at the cheaper cache-read rate
Disable streaming so the model can process more input tokens per request
rationale: Correct: retrieval sends only relevant passages, keeping requests within the window and cheaper, and prompt caching a stable document prefix cuts the cost of reusing large context. Wrong: sending the full corpus wastes tokens and can exceed limits; max_tokens governs output length and cannot expand the context window; streaming affects output delivery, not input capacity.
referenceText: For long context, provide only relevant material and use prompt caching to reuse stable large inputs efficiently; max_tokens controls output, not the context window.

stem: Which statement accurately characterizes the Message Batches API's processing behavior?
options: Batches return synchronously within a few seconds, like standard requests
Batches are processed asynchronously and target completion within 24 hours, with results retrievable for a retention window afterward
Batches are guaranteed to finish in exactly one hour every time
Batch results are deleted the instant processing finishes, so they must be streamed live
rationale: Correct: the Batches API is asynchronous, targets completion within 24 hours, and keeps results available to download for a retention window (29 days). Wrong: it is not synchronous, there is no exact one-hour guarantee, and results are not deleted immediately.
referenceText: Message batches are processed asynchronously and typically complete within 24 hours; results are available to download for 29 days.

stem: A summarization endpoint frequently returns long, rambling answers, driving up both latency and output-token cost. Which change most directly reduces output cost and latency while keeping answers on target?
options: Raise max_tokens so the model has more room to elaborate
Enable extended thinking so responses are more thorough
Constrain the output by prompting for a concise format and setting a sensible max_tokens so responses are shorter
Re-append the full source document in the assistant turn to reinforce context
rationale: Correct: output tokens are the primary driver of both latency and cost, so instructing the model to be concise and capping max_tokens directly shortens generation. Wrong: raising max_tokens permits longer, costlier output; extended thinking adds reasoning tokens and length; re-adding the source only inflates input tokens and does nothing to shorten output.
referenceText: Reducing generated output tokens via concise prompting and appropriate max_tokens lowers latency and cost, since output tokens are the primary latency driver.

stem: When integrating Claude into production code, why is it a best practice to reference a specific pinned model version (for example, a dated model ID) rather than only a floating alias?
options: Pinned versions are always billed at a lower rate than aliases
A pinned version keeps behavior stable and reproducible, so evaluations and outputs do not shift unexpectedly when a new model is released
Aliases cannot be used with the Messages API at all
Only pinned versions support tool use and streaming
rationale: Correct: pinning a specific version gives deterministic, reproducible behavior so upgrades happen on your schedule after re-evaluation, instead of changing silently when an alias advances. Wrong: pinned versions are not inherently cheaper, aliases are valid on the Messages API, and tool use and streaming are not exclusive to pinned IDs.
referenceText: Use specific model version strings for production stability; migrate deliberately and re-test when adopting a newer model.

stem: A voice assistant needs the lowest possible response latency for short conversational replies, and the tasks are not reasoning-intensive. Beyond selecting the fastest model tier, which additional technique most improves responsiveness for the end user?
options: Route each turn through the Message Batches API
Stream the response so the assistant can begin speaking as soon as the first tokens arrive
Enable extended thinking with a large token budget on every turn
Set max_tokens to the model's maximum on every reply
rationale: Correct: streaming lets the assistant start producing audio from the earliest tokens, cutting perceived latency for real-time conversation. Wrong: the Batches API is asynchronous and the opposite of low-latency; extended thinking delays first output; maxing max_tokens invites longer generations and more latency.
referenceText: For latency-sensitive applications, choose a faster model and use streaming to reduce time-to-first-token; avoid unnecessary thinking budgets.

stem: You are placing cache breakpoints for a request that contains, in order: tool definitions, a long static system prompt, and a short user question that changes every call. Which two practices maximize cache reuse? (Select 2.)
options: Order content from most static to most dynamic, keeping the changing user question at the end after the cached prefix
Place the cache_control breakpoint before the tool definitions so only the changing question is cached
Set the cache_control breakpoint at the end of the stable content (after tools and system prompt) so the shared prefix is as long as possible
Move the changing user question to the top of the prompt so it is cached first
Prepend a fresh random string to every request so the cache does not go stale
rationale: Correct: caching matches an exact prefix, so static content should come first with the breakpoint at the end of that stable region, maximizing the reusable prefix while the volatile question trails last. Wrong: caching only the changing question, moving it to the top, or injecting a random string at the top all destroy the shared prefix and prevent cache hits.
referenceText: Structure prompts with static content first and a cache breakpoint after it; dynamic content should come last to preserve a long cacheable prefix.

stem: A team runs about 5 million short product-review classifications per day, each sorting text into one of three fixed categories. Per-item latency is not critical, but total cost must be minimized. Which model is the best fit?
options: Claude Sonnet 5
Claude Haiku 4.5
Claude Opus 4.8
Claude Fable 5
rationale: Correct: Haiku 4.5 is the fastest, most cost-effective tier and is well-matched to simple high-volume classification, minimizing cost without sacrificing quality on an easy task (and pairs well with the Batches API for a further 50% discount). Wrong: Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, and Fable 5 are all more capable and more expensive than a three-way label task requires; Fable 5 is the priciest tier and the worst cost fit.
referenceText: Claude Haiku 4.5 is the fastest and most cost-effective model for simple, high-volume tasks.

stem: A developer adds cache_control to a large, unchanging system prompt, but usage.cache_read_input_tokens stays 0 across many repeated requests. What is the most likely cause?
options: The cache_control TTL was set to "1h" instead of the default 5 minutes
A datetime.now() value is interpolated into the system-prompt prefix on every request
max_tokens is set too low for the responses
The code calls client.messages.create instead of a beta messages endpoint
rationale: Correct: prompt caching is a prefix match, so any byte change in the prefix invalidates the cache from that point on; a per-request timestamp makes every prefix unique, so reads never hit. Wrong: a longer TTL only changes how long entries live (writes still occur), a low max_tokens affects output not cache reads, and caching does not require a beta endpoint.
referenceText: Prompt caching is a prefix match; any change anywhere in the prefix invalidates everything after it.

stem: A nightly job submits 40,000 independent summarization requests with no real-time latency requirement through the Message Batches API. Which statement about handling this batch is correct?
options: Results are guaranteed to return in the same order the requests were submitted
Results can return in any order, so each result must be matched to its input by custom_id
Batches cost roughly 2x standard pricing in exchange for asynchronous processing
Each batch is limited to 1,000 requests, so the job must be split into 40 batches
rationale: Correct: batch results arrive in any order and must be keyed by the per-request custom_id, never by position. Wrong: order is not guaranteed; batches cost 50% of standard pricing, not 2x; and a batch supports up to 100,000 requests (or 256 MB), so 40,000 fit in a single batch.
referenceText: Batch results arrive in any order; key each by custom_id, never by position.

stem: A request that previously used thinking={"type":"enabled","budget_tokens":8000} is being migrated to claude-opus-4-8. If the code is left unchanged, what happens, and what is the correct fix?
options: It works unchanged because budget_tokens is still supported on Opus 4.8
It returns a 400 error; migrate to thinking={"type":"adaptive"} and control depth via output_config.effort
The budget_tokens field is silently ignored, so no change is needed
It returns a 400 error; the fix is to lower budget_tokens below max_tokens
rationale: Correct: on Opus 4.8 the enabled/budget_tokens thinking shape is removed and rejected with a 400; the migration is adaptive thinking with output_config.effort to control depth. Wrong: it is not still supported and not silently ignored, and lowering budget_tokens does not help because the whole enabled/budget_tokens shape is rejected.
referenceText: thinking:{type:'enabled', budget_tokens:N} is removed and rejected with a 400 on Opus 4.8; use thinking:{type:'adaptive'} and output_config.effort.

stem: A non-streaming request sets max_tokens=100000 and intermittently fails with client-side HTTP timeouts. What is the recommended fix?
options: Raise the client's max_retries so timed-out requests are retried more often
Stream the request and assemble the result with get_final_message()/finalMessage()
Lower max_tokens to about 100 so the response returns quickly
Move the workload to the Message Batches API to avoid timeouts
rationale: Correct: large max_tokens on a non-streaming request risks SDK HTTP timeouts; the documented fix is to stream (values above ~16K should stream) and use the SDK's final-message helper. Wrong: more retries just re-incur the same timeout, dropping max_tokens to 100 truncates the intended output, and switching to Batches changes the request's semantics rather than fixing this synchronous call.
referenceText: Stream for max_tokens above ~16K; non-streaming requests hit SDK HTTP timeouts at high max_tokens.

stem: To decide whether a prompt fits the context window, a developer estimated its size with the tiktoken library. Why is this unreliable for Claude, and what is the correct approach?
options: tiktoken is accurate for Claude as long as the encoding is set to cl100k_base
tiktoken is OpenAI's tokenizer and miscounts Claude tokens; use the count_tokens endpoint with the target model
Dividing the character count by 4 gives an exact token count for any model
Token counts are identical across providers, so any tokenizer is fine
Claude has no fixed tokenizer, so exact counts are impossible before inference
rationale: Correct: tiktoken is OpenAI's tokenizer and undercounts Claude tokens, so the accurate approach is the count_tokens endpoint called with the same model ID used for inference, since counts are model-specific. Wrong: cl100k_base is still OpenAI's encoding, characters/4 is only a rough heuristic, providers' tokenizers differ, and Claude does have a defined tokenizer exposed via count_tokens.
referenceText: Do not use tiktoken; it undercounts Claude tokens. Use the count_tokens endpoint with the same model ID you'll use for inference.

stem: An agentic coding workflow on Opus 4.8 produces excellent results at output_config.effort="max" but burns more tokens per task than the budget allows. What is the best first adjustment to reduce token spend while keeping quality high?
options: Set temperature=0 to make the model more deterministic and terse
Lower output_config.effort (for example to "high"), which reduces thinking depth and token usage
Disable thinking entirely with thinking={"type":"disabled"}
Revert to thinking with an explicit budget_tokens ceiling
rationale: Correct: effort is the primary lever for the intelligence-versus-token-spend tradeoff, and stepping down from max to high (often the sweet spot) cuts token usage while retaining strong quality on agentic coding. Wrong: temperature is not a valid substitute for depth control on Opus 4.8, disabling thinking sacrifices quality on reasoning-heavy work, and budget_tokens is removed on Opus 4.8.
referenceText: effort controls thinking depth and overall token spend; lower effort means fewer, more-consolidated tool calls and less spend, with high often the sweet spot.

stem: Which two statements about prompt-cache pricing are correct? (Select 2.)
options: A 1-hour TTL cache write costs the same as a 5-minute cache write
A cache read costs about 0.1x the base input-token price
A cache read costs the same as an uncached input token
A 5-minute ephemeral cache write costs about 1.25x the base input-token price
Writing to the cache is free
rationale: Correct: a cache read costs ~0.1x base input price and a 5-minute ephemeral write costs ~1.25x base input price, the economics that make caching pay off after a couple of reads. Wrong: a 1-hour TTL write costs 2x (not the same as 5-minute), a cache read is far cheaper than uncached input, and writes are not free.
referenceText: Cache reads cost ~0.1x base input; writes cost 1.25x for 5-minute TTL and 2x for 1-hour TTL.

stem: Many requests share a large fixed preamble (instructions plus few-shot examples) but end with a different final question each time. Which practices maximize cache hits across these requests? (Select 2.)
options: Place the cache_control breakpoint at the end of the shared preamble, before the varying question
Serialize tool definitions in a stable, deterministic order across requests
Interpolate the current timestamp into the system prompt header on each request
Put the varying question before the shared preamble
Prepend a fresh UUID to the system prompt on every request
rationale: The breakpoint belongs at the end of the shared portion so every request reads the same cached prefix; placing it after the varying question would write a new entry each time and never read it. Deterministic tool ordering keeps the position-0 tools block byte-identical so it caches across requests. Timestamps, UUIDs, and putting volatile content before the stable prefix all mutate the prefix and defeat caching.
referenceText: Put the breakpoint at the end of the shared portion; keep stable content (frozen system prompt, deterministic tool list) first and volatile content after the last breakpoint.

stem: For a long-running agent, a developer must choose between compaction and context editing to manage a growing transcript. Which statement correctly distinguishes them?
options: Compaction summarizes earlier context into a summary block; context editing clears (removes) stale tool results or thinking blocks
Both features summarize the transcript in exactly the same way
Context editing summarizes older turns, while compaction deletes them without summarizing
Compaction runs entirely client-side, while context editing runs server-side
rationale: Compaction condenses earlier context into a server-side summary block (which must be preserved on subsequent requests), whereas context editing prunes stale tool results and thinking blocks by removing them rather than summarizing. They are not identical, the summarize/remove roles are not reversed, and both operate server-side via the beta Messages endpoint.
referenceText: Context editing clears old tool results or thinking blocks; it is not compaction, which summarizes.

stem: You are building a document-Q&A feature that sends the same 40,000-token knowledge base on every request, followed by a short user question that changes each time. You want to use prompt caching to reduce latency and cost. Where should you place the `cache_control` breakpoint?
options: On the final user question, so the most recently changed content is cached
On the last block of the static knowledge base, so everything up to and including it is cached and reused
On the system prompt only, because caching cannot be applied to user-turn content
On every content block, so each is cached independently
rationale: Prompt caching caches the entire prefix up to and including the block marked with `cache_control`. Marking the last static (knowledge base) block caches the large unchanging prefix while leaving the changing question after it as an uncached suffix.
